EDCS-08100450.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Gaius Pompeius Ammon.

EDCS-08100451.translation: Gaius Pompeius Compes lived 75 years and is buried here.
EDCS-08100451.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Gaius Pompeius Compes.

EDCS-08100452.translation: Quintus Pompeius Pompeianus lived 28 years; his mother made this for her son.
EDCS-08100452.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where a mother commemorates her son Quintus Pompeius Pompeianus, recording his age.

EDCS-08100453.translation: To the departed spirits. Pompeius Primus lived 80 years and is buried here.
EDCS-08100453.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits of Pompeius Primus, recording his lifespan and burial place.

EDCS-08100454.translation: To the departed spirits. Pompeius, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, [lived...]
EDCS-08100454.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits of Pompeius, son of Caius, mentioning his tribe and incomplete lifespan details.

EDCS-08100455.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pompeia Festiva lived 55 years and is buried here.
EDCS-08100455.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits of Pompeia Festiva, recording her lifespan and burial place.

EDCS-08100456.translation: To the departed spirits. Pompeia Nigrosa lived 65 years.
EDCS-08100456.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed spirits of Pompeia Nigrosa, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-08100457.translation: Pompeia Novicia lived 50 years, loyal to her friends, kind to her parents, and is buried here.
EDCS-08100457.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and virtues of Pompeia Novicia, who is buried here.

EDCS-08100458.translation: Gaius Popilius Tiro lived 53 years; bones interred.
EDCS-08100458.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Gaius Popilius Tiro.

EDCS-08100459.translation: Publius Porcius Faustus lived 95 years; he rests here.
EDCS-08100459.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publius Porcius Faustus.

EDCS-08100461.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Propertius Barbarus.

EDCS-08100462.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publia Propertia lived 27 years.
EDCS-08100462.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publia Propertia.

EDCS-08100463.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publicius Matas lived 97 years; he rests here.
EDCS-08100463.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publicius Matas.

EDCS-08100464.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publicius Maximus lived 60 years.
EDCS-08100464.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publicius Maximus.

EDCS-08100466.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Publicia Adventa lived 35 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100466.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publicia Adventa.

EDCS-08100467.translation: In memory of Publicia Pacia who lived 30 years.
EDCS-08100467.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Publicia Pacia.

EDCS-08100471.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed; Quintia Cyril[la] lived 31 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100471.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Quintia Cyril[la].

EDCS-08100473.translation: In memory of Romani who lived 2 years, 5 months, 22 days; his mother Ianuaria.
EDCS-08100473.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Romani and is set up by his mother Ianuaria.

EDCS-08100474.translation: Roscia Maxima, daughter of Sex., lived 10 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100474.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Roscia Maxima, who died at age 10.

EDCS-08100475.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the age of Rusticinus, who died at age 13.

EDCS-08100477.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sallustia Rogata lived 21 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100477.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Sallustia Rogata, who died at age 21.

EDCS-08100478.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sallustia Saturnina lived 25 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100478.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Sallustia Saturnina, who died at age 25.

EDCS-08100479.translation: Salsa lived 30 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100479.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Salsa, who died at age 30.

EDCS-08100480.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Salvidenia Luciosia lived 35 years; she rests here.
EDCS-08100480.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Salvidenia Luciosia, who died at age 35.

EDCS-08100481.translation: Salvidenia Marcellina lived 111 years; may your bones fare well.
EDCS-08100481.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Salvidenia Marcellina, who died at age 111.

EDCS-08100482.text_edition: \n Samsucanae Papiria Quieta sorori carissimae posuit vixit annos LXV\n
EDCS-08100482.translation: Papiria Quieta to Samsucanae, her dearest sister, erected this; she lived 65 years.
EDCS-08100482.summary: This inscription is a tombstone set up by Papiria Quieta for her sister, recording the sister's age at death as 65.

EDCS-08100483.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Scribonius Cosmus lived 80 years; he rests here.
EDCS-08100483.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Lucius Scribonius Cosmus, who died at age 80.

EDCS-08100485.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Sempronius Candidus lived 101 years; he rests here.
EDCS-08100485.summary: This inscription is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Lucius Sempronius Candidus, who died at age 101.

EDCS-08100486.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Sempronia Peculiaris, also called Pampamilla, lived for 41 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100486.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sempronia Peculiaris, recording her age at death and her burial place.

EDCS-08100487.translation: Quintus Sittius Exactor, son, lived for 25 years.
EDCS-08100487.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Sittius Exactor, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08100488.translation: To the memory of Quintus Sittius Gemellus, a brave and good boy. Quintus Sittius Gemellus the father and Iulia Urbana set this up for their dearest son. He lived for 13 years and 10 months. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08100488.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their young son, Quintus Sittius Gemellus, recording his age at death and expressing good wishes.

EDCS-08100489.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Publius Sittius Ianuarius lived for 80 years. He rests here.
EDCS-08100489.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Sittius Ianuarius, recording his age at death and his burial place.

EDCS-08100490.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Sittius Martialis lived for 100 years. He rests here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08100490.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittius Martialis, recording his great age at death and his burial place, with a wish for his repose.

EDCS-08100491.translation: To the gods of the underworld. He rests here. Sittius Primus lived for 70 years.
EDCS-08100491.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittius Primus, recording his age at death and his burial place.

EDCS-08100492.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Lucius Sittius Rusticus lived for 30 years. He rests here.
EDCS-08100492.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Sittius Rusticus, recording his age at death and his burial place.

EDCS-08100493.translation: Quintus Sittius Victor lived for 14 years. He rests here.
EDCS-08100493.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Sittius Victor, recording his age at death and his burial place.

EDCS-08100494.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Sittius Vitalis lived for 20 years.
EDCS-08100494.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittius Vitalis, recording his age at death.

EDCS-08100495.translation: Sittia Exorata lived for 80 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100495.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Exorata, recording her age at death and her burial place.

EDCS-08100497.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittia Felix lived 5 years. May she rest well here.
EDCS-08100497.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Felix, who died at the age of five.

EDCS-08100498.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittia Gemelina lived 18 years.
EDCS-08100498.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Gemelina, who died at the age of eighteen.

EDCS-08100499.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittia Monula lived 60 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100499.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Monula, who died at the age of sixty.

EDCS-08100500.translation: To the departed spirits. Sittia Mulsulae lived 25 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100500.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Mulsulae, who died at the age of twenty-five.

EDCS-08100501.translation: Sittia Mustia lived 60 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100501.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Mustia, who died at the age of sixty.

EDCS-08100503.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucia Sittia Rustica lived 30 years. She rests here.
EDCS-08100503.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucia Sittia Rustica, who died at the age of thirty.

EDCS-08100504.translation: To the departed spirits. For Sosius Fructus, a most holy and most dutiful man, who lived [X] years. Successus (?), his brother, made this.
EDCS-08100504.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Successus for his brother Sosius Fructus, praising his character.

EDCS-08100506.translation: Publius Tarquinius Philodespotus lived 25 years. He rests here.
EDCS-08100506.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Tarquinius Philodespotus, who died at the age of twenty-five.

EDCS-08100507.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Tatius Felix lived 45 years. Sittia Maxima placed this for her husband.
EDCS-08100507.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Tatius Felix, erected by his wife Sittia Maxima.

EDCS-08100509.translation: To the departed spirits. Titinius Episucus lived 62 years.
EDCS-08100509.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titinius Episucus, who died at the age of sixty-two.

EDCS-08100510.translation: Marcus Titinius Pontesis lived 91 years; he is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08100510.summary: A tombstone recording the life and burial of Marcus Titinius Pontesis, with a common wish for the deceased.

EDCS-08100511.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tituria Venefria.
EDCS-08100511.summary: A funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed for Tituria Venefria.

EDCS-08100512.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Trebia Contenta lived 95 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-08100512.summary: A tombstone for Trebia Contenta, recording her age and burial.

EDCS-08100513.translation: In memory of Tutia Eutychia, who was also called Lucilla, lived 40 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-08100513.summary: A memorial for Tutia Eutychia, also known as Lucilla, recording her age and burial.

EDCS-08100514.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valeria Sittoria lived 85 years.
EDCS-08100514.summary: A funerary dedication for Valeria Sittoria, recording her age.

EDCS-08100515.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vasidia Saturnina lived 35 years.
EDCS-08100515.summary: A funerary dedication for Vasidia Saturnina, recording her age.

EDCS-08100516.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Veneria lived 12 years.
EDCS-08100516.summary: A funerary dedication for Veneria, recording her age.

EDCS-08100517.translation: Quintus Vettius Saturninus lived 24 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08100517.summary: A tombstone recording the life and burial of Quintus Vettius Saturninus.

EDCS-08100520.translation: Vibieia Severila, daughter of Caius, lived 8 years; she is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08100520.summary: A tombstone for Vibieia Severila, daughter of Caius, recording her age, burial, and a wish for her repose.

EDCS-08100522.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vitalis lived 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08100522.summary: A tombstone for Vitalis, recording his age and burial.

EDCS-08100524.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Volumnius Ianuarius lived for 55 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08100524.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and age of Lucius Volumnius Ianuarius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08100525.translation: Publius Volumnius Novellus lived for 95 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08100525.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and age of Publius Volumnius Novellus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08100526.translation: Caius Volussius Felix lived for 87 years.
EDCS-08100526.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and age of Caius Volussius Felix, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08100527.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred. Quintus Volusius Fortunatus, dutiful, lived for 55 years.
EDCS-08100527.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death and age of Quintus Volusius Fortunatus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08300572.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate V consule III patre patriae Publius Metilius Secundus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-08300572.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, Publius Metilius Secundus, imperial legate with praetorian power, patron of the colony, dedicated [this] by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-08300572.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public work by Publius Metilius Secundus, imperial legate, under Emperor Hadrian (reigned 117-138 AD).

EDCS-08300575.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote divi Nervae pronepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patre patriae imperatore II pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XIIII consule IIII plateam stratam Marcus Valerius Etruscus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-08300575.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the divine Hadrian, grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the divine Nerva, father of the country, holding tribunician power for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, Marcus Valerius Etruscus, imperial legate with praetorian power, patron of the colony, dedicated the paved street by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-08300575.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a paved street by Marcus Valerius Etruscus, imperial legate, under Emperor Antoninus Pius (reigned 138-161 AD).

EDCS-08600659.text_edition: \n Chioni memoriae Publi Exoppi Publi filii Quirina Nivalis quaestoris aedilis IIIviri quinquennalis omnibusque honoribus IIII coloniarum functi vixit annos LXXV Chioni Exoppia Saturnina patri incomparabili\n
EDCS-08600659.translation: To the memory of Chion. Publius Exoppius Nivalis, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, who held the offices of quaestor, aedile three times, quinquennial duovir, and served in all other offices in four colonies, lived for 75 years. To Chion, his incomparable father, Exoppia Saturnina [erected this].
EDCS-08600659.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument set up by Exoppia Saturnina for her father, Publius Exoppius Nivalis, a prominent local official, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08600660.text_edition: \n Marco Pontio Marci filio Pupinia Varano Sabino tribuno laticlavio militum legionis III Augustae XVviro sacris faciundis Xviro stlitibus iudicandis VIviro turmae equitum Romanorum signiferi legionis eiusdem\n
EDCS-08600660.translation: To Marcus Pontius Varus Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Pupinia tribe, military tribune with senatorial rank of the 3rd Augustan Legion, member of the college of fifteen for performing sacred rites, member of the college of ten for judging lawsuits, decurion of a troop of Roman knights, standard-bearer of the same legion.
EDCS-08600660.summary: This inscription lists the extensive military and civil offices held by Marcus Pontius Varus Sabinus, a Roman military officer, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08600661.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae pro salute Imperatorum dominorum nostrorum Invictissimorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini Augustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti et senatus et castrorum curia Commodiana curiales curiae Commodianae Lucius Maenius Vibidianus Marcus Annius Faustus Caius Popilius Laetus Lucius Longeiius sic Primus Lucius Licinius Honoratus Marcus Volussenius Fortunatus Quintus Caecilius Ateanus Quintus Timinius Felix Quintus Lurius Severus Publius Caelius Saturninus Tiberius Claudius Victor Lucius Iulius Donatus magister Lucius Statius Maximus Marcus Cornelius Servandianus Caius Clodius Saturninus Lucius Aulonius Felix Lucius Valerius Victor Caius Albanius Aelianus Publius Aelius Victor Marcus Aennius Saturninus Leporinus Marcus Acilius Lucanus Quintus Sallustius Candidus Quintus Iulius Thelemachus Quintus Helvius Muricus Quintus Iulius Mychronius Caius Publilius Fortunatus Marcus Ulpius Felix Lucius Aedius Ianuarius Lucius Caeladius Saturninus Quintus Grattius Fortunatus Marcus Antonius Saturninus Lucius Egnatius Agricola Marcus Ulpius Peculiaris Publius Pactimeius Felix Quintus Antonius Sulla Marcus Acilius Celadianus Publius Fabius Catulianus Quintus Iulius Sapa Lucius Aemilius Victor Caius Annius Posianus Lucius Claudius Festivus Titus Flavius Cuminus Lucius Aemilius Donatus Quintus Pomponius Geminus Quintus Grattius Donatus Lucius Blossius Honoratus Quintus Cornelius Victor Lucius Valerius Felix Quintus Iulius Fortunatus Publius Camerius Faustus Caius Peticius Castus Quintus Arrius Processus Stablicus\n
EDCS-08600661.translation: To Diana Augusta, for the safety of our lords, the most invincible emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the emperor and the senate and the camps, and the Commodian curia, and the curiales of the Commodian curia, [and many named individuals, including] Lucius Maenius Vibidianus, master, Lucius Statius Maximus, Marcus Cornelius Servandianus, Caius Clodius Saturninus, Lucius Aulonius Felix, Lucius Valerius Victor, Caius Albanius Aelianus, Publius Aelius Victor, Marcus Aennius Saturninus Leporinus, Marcus Acilius Lucanus, Quintus Sallustius Candidus, Quintus Julius Thelemachus, Quintus Helvius Muricus, Quintus Julius Mychronius, Caius Publilius Fortunatus, Marcus Ulpius Felix, Lucius Aedius Ianuarius, Lucius Caeladius Saturninus, Quintus Grattius Fortunatus, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, Lucius Egnatius Agricola, Marcus Ulpius Peculiaris, Publius Pactimeius Felix, Quintus Antonius Sulla, Marcus Acilius Celadianus, Publius Fabius Catulianus, Quintus Julius Sapa, Lucius Aemilius Victor, Caius Annius Posianus, Lucius Claudius Festivus, Titus Flavius Cuminus, Lucius Aemilius Donatus, Quintus Pomponius Geminus, Quintus Grattius Donatus, Lucius Blossius Honoratus, Quintus Cornelius Victor, Lucius Valerius Felix, Quintus Julius Fortunatus, Publius Camerius Faustus, Caius Peticius Castus, Quintus Arrius Processus Stablicus.
EDCS-08600661.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana Augusta made by a large group of named individuals, including curiales and a magister, for the safety of the emperors Severus and Caracalla and Empress Julia Domna, likely dating to their joint rule (c. 198-211 AD).

EDCS-08901192.text_edition: \n Publius Iulius Publi filius Papiria Liberalis sacerdotalis provinciae Africae flamen perpetuus quinquennalis IIvir praefectus iure dicundo quaestor et in colonia Thysdritana flamen perpetuus lacum quem super legitimam flamoni summam promiserat ex HS XXXII CCCXLVIII fecit idemque dedicavit decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08901192.translation: Publius Julius Liberalis, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, permanent priest of the province of Africa, perpetual priest, quinquennial duovir, prefect for administering justice, quaestor, and in the colony of Thysdrus, perpetual priest, constructed the lake which he promised above the legal amount for the priesthood with 32,348 sesterces, and dedicated it himself by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08901192.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a lake by Publius Julius Liberalis, a high-ranking local official in Roman Africa, who held numerous priestly and magistratorial positions, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08901193.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo conservatori domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Quinti Anici Fausti legati Augusti pro praetore AED IPAN Severo et Herenniano consulibus Acilius Felix Imperatore Commodo II et Vero consulibus Iulius Saim Imperatore Commodo V consule Aemilius Saturninus Falco et Claro consulibus Iulius Donatus Geminius Emeritus Tertullo et Clemente consulibus Arrius \n
EDCS-08901193.translation: To Jupiter, the Best and Greatest, protector of our lord the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and of Quintus Anicius Faustus, imperial legate with praetorian power, under the consulship of Aedippan Severus and Herennianus, and under the consulship of Emperor Commodus II and Verus, and of Acilius Felix, and under the consulship of Emperor Commodus V, and of Aemilius Saturninus Falco and Claros, and under the consulship of Julius Donatus Geminius Emeritus Tertullus and Clemens, Arrius.
EDCS-08901193.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by various officials, including an imperial legate and consuls, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-09001592.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Au<r=B>e<l=T>io Diocletiano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto \n
EDCS-09001592.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Aurelius Diocletianus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-09001592.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, a Roman emperor from the late 3rd and early 4th centuries AD.

EDCS-09001597.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Invicto Pio Felici Augusto Pio maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli II proconsuli et Herenniae Etruscillae coniugi Augusti nostri res publica coloniae Thamugadensium \n
EDCS-09001597.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, twice consul, proconsul, and to Herennia Etruscilla, wife of our Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-09001597.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadi to Emperor Trajan Decius and his wife Herennia Etruscilla, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001598.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar [------
EDCS-09001598.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Julius Philippus Caesar, who was a Caesar and later Emperor in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001599.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Invicto Pio \n
EDCS-09001599.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, the Invincible, Pious.
EDCS-09001599.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, who reigned in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001602.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimo Caesari Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Iuli Veri Maximini Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate consulis patris patriae proconsulis filio res publica coloniae Thamugadensium II\n
EDCS-09001602.translation: To Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, son of the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the state of the colony of Thamugadensium, twice.
EDCS-09001602.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadensium to Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, son of Emperor Maximinus Thrax, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001603.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Iulio Philippo Invicto Pio Felice Augusto res publica coloniae Thamugadensium ]\n
EDCS-09001603.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadensium.
EDCS-09001603.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadensium to Emperor Philippus Arabs, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001604.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari Augusto res publica coloniae Thamugadensium\n
EDCS-09001604.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadensium.
EDCS-09001604.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadensium to Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001606.text_edition: \n ------ Cae]sari Carino Augusto res publica coloniae Thamugadensium milia passuum \n
EDCS-09001606.translation: [------ Caesar]inus Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadensium, miles.
EDCS-09001606.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadensium to an emperor whose name ends in -inus, likely Carinus, and mentions miles (soldiers or miles).

EDCS-09001608.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Pio Felice Augusto Parthico maximo Britannico maximo Germanico maximo pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XVIII imperatore III consule IIII patre patriae proconsule et Iulia Augusta matre Augusti et castrorum et senatus ac patriae a Lambaese milia passuum V\n
EDCS-09001608.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 18th time, Imperator for the 3rd time, Consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and of the camp and of the senate and of the fatherland, 5 miles from Lambaesis.
EDCS-09001608.summary: This inscription records distances from Lambaesis, mentioning Emperor Caracalla and his mother Julia Domna, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09001609.text_edition: \n Perpetuo nobilissimo Constantino Caesari Augusto res publica coloniae Lambaesitanorum \n
EDCS-09001609.translation: To the eternal, most noble Caesar Augustus Constantine, for the well-being of the state of the colony of Lambesis.
EDCS-09001609.summary: This is a public dedication by the colony of Lambesis to Emperor Constantine, likely commemorating a benefit or honor bestowed by him.

EDCS-09001615.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Domitio Aurelliano sic Invicto Pio Felici \n
EDCS-09001615.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-09001615.summary: This is a formal acclamation and epithet for Emperor Aurelian, likely part of a larger inscription praising his rule.

EDCS-09001621.text_edition: \n Perpetuo victoriosissimo indulgentissimo Imperatori restitutori orbis Lucio Domitio Aureliano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici [maximo tribunicia potestate ------\n
EDCS-09001621.translation: To the eternal, most victorious, most indulgent Emperor, restorer of the world, Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for [duration].
EDCS-09001621.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Aurelian, detailing his titles and achievements as a 'restorer of the world', likely dating to his reign.

EDCS-09300564.text_edition: \n Genio TFLZI Augusto pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti fili eius Quintus Homullius Victor et Caius Apuleius Gratianus magistri sua pecunia donum dederunt Laterano et Rufino consulibus\n
EDCS-09300564.translation: For the genius of Augustus, for the health of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Augustus and his son Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Quintus Homullius Victor and Caius Apuleius Gratianus, magistrates, gave this gift at their own expense. Consuls Lateranus and Rufinus.
EDCS-09300564.summary: This dedication, made at private expense by two magistrates, is for the health of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, and is dated by the consulship of Lateranus and Rufinus (likely 197 or 199 AD).

EDCS-09401253.text_edition: \n In pace Flavius Vitalis Vitarit vixit decessit per quinos semis annos depos(itus) III Nonas Apriles\n
EDCS-09401253.translation: In peace. Flavius Vitalis. He lived and died for fifteen and a half years. Buried on the 3rd day before the Nones of April.
EDCS-09401253.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Flavius Vitalis, recording his age at death and burial date, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-09401645.text_edition: \n Diis bonis numinibus praesentibus Aesculapio et Saluti sacrum Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-09401645.translation: Sacred to the good gods and present divine powers, Aesculapius and Salus. Aurelius Decimus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, from the rank of peregrine troops, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09401645.summary: This is a votive inscription to Aesculapius and Salus by Aurelius Decimus, a high-ranking provincial governor, fulfilling a vow, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09401650.text_edition: \n Mauris Augustis Lucius Caecilius Felix votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-09401650.translation: To the August Mauri. Lucius Caecilius Felix fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-09401650.summary: This is a simple votive inscription by Lucius Caecilius Felix to the 'August Mauri', likely local deities or spirits.

EDCS-09700396.text_edition: \n Sabino et Venusto Luceius Felix vixit annos LXVIIII heredes patri fecerunt\n
EDCS-09700396.translation: Sabinus and Venustus. Luceius Felix lived 69 years. His heirs made this for their father.
EDCS-09700396.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Luceius Felix, stating his age at death and that it was erected by his heirs, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-09700782.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus thermas vetustate conlabsas sic per legionem suam III Augustam restituit curante Iulio Fortunatiano viro clarissimo consulari comite et legato Augusti et Aurelio Syro viro egregio praefecto legionis\n
EDCS-09700782.translation: Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus restored the baths, collapsed by age, with his Legio III Augusta. Under the supervision of Julius Fortunatianus, a most illustrious man, consular count, and legate of Augustus, and Aurelius Syrus, an excellent man, prefect of the legion.
EDCS-09700782.summary: This inscription records Emperor Gallienus' restoration of public baths using his Legio III Augusta, overseen by imperial officials, likely from his reign (253-268 AD).

EDCS-09700785.text_edition: \n Deo magno sacrum Caius Gargilius Zabo intravit sacerdos Saturni annis LXV votum libens animo solvit pecora VIIII agnum taurum verbecem oviculam gallinas V\n
EDCS-09700785.translation: Sacred to the Great God. Caius Gargilius Zabo, priest of Saturn, entered [his office/service] at 65 years old and willingly fulfilled his vow, including 9 animals: a bull calf, a ram, a ewe, and 5 hens.
EDCS-09700785.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Gargilius Zabo, priest of Saturn, recording his age when taking up his office and the offerings made, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700786.text_edition: \n Martyires sic benedicti in menite habete Quintianum\n
EDCS-09700786.translation: Remember the martyrs blessed in this place, Quintianus.
EDCS-09700786.summary: This inscription is a reminder to remember martyrs, mentioning Quintianus, likely from the early Christian period.

EDCS-09700787.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Alexandri Augusti Pii Felicis collegium Maiorum et Minorum posuit dedicavit bonis bene\n
EDCS-09700787.translation: For Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Alexander Augustus Pius Felix, the association of elders and juniors erected and dedicated this with good works.
EDCS-09700787.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by a collegium for the safety of Emperor Alexander Severus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701271.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae proconsuli res publica castelli Tidditanorum numini maiestatique eius devota pecunia publica decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-09701271.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the public affairs of the town of Tiddi, devoted to his divine majesty, from public funds by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09701271.summary: This inscription records a decree by the town of Tiddi, using public funds, to honor Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701273.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Vespasiani Caesaris Augusti agri publici Cirtensium adsignati Suburburibus Regianis et Nicibibus per Tullium Pomponianum Capitonem legatum Augusti\n
EDCS-09701273.translation: By authority of the Emperor Caesar Augustus Vespasian, public lands of the Cirta region assigned to the Suburbicarii Regiani and Nicibes, by Tullius Pomponianus Capito, legate of Augustus.
EDCS-09701273.summary: This inscription records the assignment of public lands in the Cirta region by the legate Tullius Pomponianus Capito under the authority of Emperor Vespasian, likely from the late 1st century CE.

EDCS-09701284.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli II nepoti divorum Gordianorum\n
EDCS-09701284.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the 2nd time, grandson of the divine Gordians.
EDCS-09701284.summary: This inscription is an honorific titulature for Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09701285.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Nervae filio Nerva Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consule III patre patriae Lucio Munatio Gallo legato Augusti pro praetore XVI\n
EDCS-09701285.translation: While Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, with Lucius Munatius Gallus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, for the 16th time.
EDCS-09701285.summary: This inscription records consular and tribunician dating for Emperor Trajan, with the legate Lucius Munatius Gallus, likely from the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09800385.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio Augusto sacrum pro aeternitate imperi Gordiani Augusti nostri Cosinius Marcianus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro [praetore ------\n
EDCS-09800385.translation: Sacred to the divine Mercury, for the eternity of the empire of our lord Gordianus Augustus, Cosinius Marcianus, vir clarissimus, legate of Augustus pro praetore [on behalf of...].
EDCS-09800385.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mercury by the legate Cosinius Marcianus for the eternity of the empire of Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800387.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Medico Parthico Germanico patri patriae imperatori VII tribunicia potestate XXVI consuli III dedicante Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore plateam Lambaesitani straverunt decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-09800387.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Germanicus, father of the fatherland, imperator for the 7th time, holder of tribunician power for the 26th time, consul for the 3rd time, with Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, the people of Lambaesis paved the street by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-09800387.summary: This inscription records the paving of a street in Lambaesis by the decurions, honoring Emperor Caracalla and featuring the legate Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800389.text_edition: \n Pro salute et incolumitate domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Antonini Augusti Pii Felicis et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti et castrorum et senatus et patriae cultores dei Ierhobolis iuniores constituti Praesente et Extricato II consulibus Idibus Martis fecerunt magistri cultorum qui nomina sua subiecerunt dedicaverunt ius Victor [ ------\n
EDCS-09800389.translation: For the safety and well-being of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix and Julia Augusta, mother of the emperor and of the camp and of the senate and of the fatherland, the devotees of the god Ierobolis the Younger, with Praesens and Extricatus as consuls, on the Ides of March, the magistrates of the devotees, who placed their names below, dedicated [this...].
EDCS-09800389.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the devotees of a god for the safety of Emperor Elagabalus and Julia Domna, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800391.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, legate.
EDCS-09800391.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, a legate, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800392.text_edition: \n Lucio Vespronio Candido Sallustio Sabiniano consuli speculatores et beneficiarii eius\n
EDCS-09800392.translation: To Lucius Vespronius Candidus Sallustius Sabinianus, consul, his speculatores and beneficiarii.
EDCS-09800392.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the speculatores and beneficiarii (soldiers or attendants) of a consul, Lucius Vespronius Candidus Sallustius Sabinianus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800394.text_edition: \n miniae Verae coniugi Prisci legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati speculatores et beneficiari\n
EDCS-09800394.translation: To Minia Vera, wife of Priscus, legatus Augusti pro praetore and designated consul, from his speculatores and beneficiarii.
EDCS-09800394.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the speculatores and beneficiarii of Priscus, who held the offices of legatus Augusti pro praetore and designated consul, honoring his wife, Minia Vera, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800398.text_edition: \n Dedicata V Nonas Maias Aemiliano et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-09800398.translation: Dedicated on the fifth day before the Nones of May, in the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus.
EDCS-09800398.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument or object on a specific date, May 3rd, during the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800402.text_edition: \n Publio Licinio Gallieno nobilissimo Caesari Augusto\n
EDCS-09800402.translation: To Publius Licinius Gallienus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-09800402.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication to Publius Licinius Gallienus, who was declared Caesar and later Augustus, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800403.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Caro Invicto Pio Felici Augusto et Carino nobilissimo Caesari Augusto Germanico maximo et Imperatori Marco Aurelio Numeriano nobilissimo Caesari Augusto\n
EDCS-09800403.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus, the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, and to Carinus, most noble Caesar Augustus, greatest conqueror of the Germans, and to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Numerian, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-09800403.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication to three emperors: Carus, Carinus, and Numerian, dating to their period of joint rule in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800618.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Of Publius Julius Marcellus, a most simple man, who lived 90 years, is buried here. Iulia Matutina [placed this for her] father.
EDCS-09800618.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Julius Marcellus, a man who lived to 90 years old, set up by his daughter Iulia Matutina, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800676.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Magnius Orfeitus(?), son of Lucius, lived 28 years.
EDCS-09800676.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Magnius Orfeitus(?), who died at the age of 28, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800692.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Of Aufidius Euphrosynus, who lived 60 years, is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-09800692.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aufidius Euphrosynus, who died at the age of 60, with a wish for his peaceful rest, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800693.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Cornelia Lucida. Urbanus [erected this for his] wife, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-09800693.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelia Lucida, who died at the age of 60, set up by her husband Urbanus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800694.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fabius Gallus Oxten(?), who died at the age of 63, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800695.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Galerius lived for approximately SFMA (?) years.
EDCS-09800695.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Marcus Galerius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800698.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Iulia Iuliosa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800699.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Mar<i=S>(a)e(?) <C=D>/(e)cil(i)ae, who lived for 121 years.
EDCS-09800699.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Mar<i=S>(a)e(?) <C=D>/(e)cil(i)ae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800701.translation: Marcus Claudius(?) Musteolus lived for 85 years.
EDCS-09800701.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Marcus Claudius(?) Musteolus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800702.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Nonius Crescens lived for 90 years.
EDCS-09800702.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Nonius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800706.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Publius Antonius Publi filius Quirina Antoninus aedilis designatus signum marmoreum cultoribus Dianae et Mercuri sua pecunia dedit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-09800706.translation: Sacred to Diana Augusta. Publius Antonius Antoninus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, designated aedile, gave this marble statue for Diana and Mercury to their worshippers with his own money, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-09800706.summary: This is a dedication of a statue to Diana Augusta by Publius Antonius Antoninus, who was a designated aedile, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800710.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Gaius Julius as an aedile, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800711.text_edition: \n Lucius Manilius Luci filius Quirina filiorum suorum po[su------\n
EDCS-09800711.translation: Lucius Manilius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, for his sons set up [this monument...]
EDCS-09800711.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Lucius Manilius, of the Quirina tribe, set up a monument for his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800712.text_edition: \n Quinto Bottio Quinti filio Quirina Senecioni filio\n
EDCS-09800712.translation: For Quintus Bottius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, and for his son Senecio.
EDCS-09800712.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Quintus Bottius and his son Senecio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800713.summary: This inscription likely records a donation or expenditure of 10,000 sesterces by Quirina Senecio, possibly related to a funerary monument, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800714.text_edition: \n Caio Bottio filio Quirina centioni VE \n
EDCS-09800714.translation: To Gaius Bottius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, centurion, may you fare well.
EDCS-09800714.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a centurion named Gaius Bottius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800719.text_edition: \n s Cai fili- Quirina C I promisit ex \n
EDCS-09800719.translation: Gaius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, promised from...
EDCS-09800719.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording a promise made by Gaius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, likely related to legal or financial matters.

EDCS-09800724.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius H Cai filius Quirina Paganus magisterii fori honore bis functus vixit annos C hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800724.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius H. Gaius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, Paganus, having twice held the office of magistrate of the forum, lived 100 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09800724.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Paganus, who lived to 100 and held the office of forum magistrate twice, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800725.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum cilia LGAI ca rarissimII Quintus Avianius Emeritus miles legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-09800725.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Avianius Emeritus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, who was very dear...
EDCS-09800725.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Quintus Avianius Emeritus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-09800726.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius A F annos vixit XXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800726.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius, son of Aulus, lived 25 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09800726.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius, son of Aulus, who died at the age of 25, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800727.text_edition: \n Quintus A Sterculus vixit annos CI hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800727.translation: Quintus A. Sterculus lived 101 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09800727.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus A. Sterculus, who lived to 101, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800728.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aboia Geminia vixit annis LXXXI hic sita est\n
EDCS-09800728.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aboia Geminia lived 81 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-09800728.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aboia Geminia, who lived to 81, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800729.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Acilius Martialis vixit annos VIIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800729.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Acilius Martialis lived 9 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09800729.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Acilius Martialis, who died at the age of 9, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800730.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Acilius Subitanus vixit annos LV hic situs\n
EDCS-09800730.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Acilius Subitanus lived 55 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-09800730.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Acilius Subitanus, who lived to 55, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800731.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Acilius Victor vixit annos LI hic situs est ossa tibi bene quiescant\n
EDCS-09800731.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Acilius Victor lived 51 years. He is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-09800731.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Acilius Victor, who lived to 51, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09800732.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Acilia Domestica vixit annos LXXV hic sita est\n
EDCS-09800732.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Acilia Domestica lived 75 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-09800732.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Acilia Domestica.

EDCS-09800733.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Acilia Natalis vixit annos XXXXV\n
EDCS-09800733.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Acilia Natalis lived 45 years.
EDCS-09800733.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span of Acilia Natalis.

EDCS-09800734.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Acilia Venusta vixit annos XXXXI hic sita est\n
EDCS-09800734.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Acilia Venusta lived 41 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-09800734.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Acilia Venusta.

EDCS-09800735.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Acilia Cai filia Quirina Vitalis vixit annos LV hic sita est\n
EDCS-09800735.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Acilia Vitalis, daughter of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 55 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-09800735.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Acilia Vitalis, specifying her father and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-09800736.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Aedinius Gaiolus vixit annos LXXXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800736.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Aedinius Gaiolus lived 90 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800736.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Gaius Aedinius Gaiolus.

EDCS-09800737.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Aedinius Martia<l=C>is hic <e=L>(st) situs vixit annos LX \n
EDCS-09800737.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Aedinius Martialis lived 60 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800737.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Gaius Aedinius Martialis.

EDCS-09800738.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aedinia Hospita vixit annos LXV\n
EDCS-09800738.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aedinia Hospita lived 65 years.
EDCS-09800738.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span of Aedinia Hospita.

EDCS-09800739.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Aelius Publianus vixit annos LXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800739.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Aelius Publianus lived 70 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800739.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Publius Aelius Publianus.

EDCS-09800741.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Aemilius Acinus vixit annos LI hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800741.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Aemilius Acinus lived 51 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800741.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Gaius Aemilius Acinus.

EDCS-09800742.text_edition: \n Caius Aemilius Buturarius vixit annos XXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800742.translation: Gaius Aemilius Buturarius lived 25 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800742.summary: A funerary inscription recording the life span and burial of Gaius Aemilius Buturarius.

EDCS-09800743.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Aemilius Copo vixit annos XXXV\n
EDCS-09800743.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Aemilius Copo lived for 35 years.
EDCS-09800743.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Quintus Aemilius Copo, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09800744.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Aemilius Dexter vixit annos LX hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800744.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Aemilius Dexter lived for 60 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800744.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan and burial of Caius Aemilius Dexter, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09800745.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Aemilius Quinti libertus Quirina Felix vixit annos LXXXI hic situs est\n
EDCS-09800745.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Aemilius Quirina Felix, freedman of Quintus Aemilius, lived for 81 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-09800745.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan and burial of Quintus Aemilius Quirina Felix, a freedman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09801951.text_edition: \n Genio populi sacrum ex testamento Quinti Lepti Musteoli Iulius Nampulus Marcus Iulius Rogatianus Caius Iulius Vitalis fratres et coheredes et heredes Iuli Maximi coheredis eorum super HS III milia nummum quae testamento fieri decrevit additis HS nummis sic Imille posuerunt idemque dedicaverunt Locus datus Kalendis Mais Messalla et Sabino consulibus dicata isdem Idibus Mais\n
EDCS-09801951.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the people, in accordance with the will of Quintus Leptus Musteolus. Iulius Nampulus, Marcus Iulius Rogatianus, and Caius Iulius Vitalis, brothers and co-heirs and heirs of their co-heir Iulius Maximus, have erected and dedicated this, with an additional 1000 sesterces to the 3000 sesterces that he decreed in his will should be spent. The site was granted on the Kalends of May in the consulship of Messalla and Sabinus, and dedicated on the Ides of May of the same year.
EDCS-09801951.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a monument by three brothers and co-heirs, fulfilling the will of their deceased co-heir Iulius Maximus, dated to May 532 AUC (AD 79).

EDCS-11600299.text_edition: \n Corfidius Crementius flamen perpetuus avorum atavorumque morum probitate auctis insignibus erga publica privataque prepollens domum comparavit in umbilico sitam patrie ruinis iam diu informibus tristem felicius quam condita est restituit et adiecto decori in aeternum robore sibi posterisque laetioribus dedit Corfidiorum\n
EDCS-11600299.translation: Corfidius Crementius, perpetual priest, distinguished by his ancestors' and forefathers' integrity, and preeminent in public and private affairs, acquired a house situated in the heart of the city, which had long been sadly disfigured by ruins. He restored it more successfully than it had been built and, adding to its beauty, dedicated it for himself and his happier descendants in eternal strength.
EDCS-11600299.summary: This inscription records the restoration and dedication of a house by Corfidius Crementius, a perpetual priest, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-12500152.text_edition: \n Maximo II et Urbano consulibus Bacaci Augusto sacrum pridie Kalendas Apriles Caius Sittius Stephanus et Quintus Iulius II QI\n
EDCS-12500152.translation: Sacred to Apollo Augustus, on the day before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Maximinus II and Urbanus.
EDCS-12500152.summary: This is a dedication to Apollo Augustus, dated to the consulship of Maximinus II and Urbanus (likely AD 236).

EDCS-12500224.translation: To Earth Mother, Mother of Magna Mater Idaea. Publius Sextilius, son of Caius, of the Voltinia tribe, Honoratus, offered and made a bull and a sheep sacrifice, and erected an altar.
EDCS-12500224.summary: This inscription records a votive offering and altar erection by Publius Sextilius Honoratus to Earth Mother, Magna Mater Idaea, in the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12500238.translation: To Julia Seia Herennia Sallustia Barbia Orbiaana Augusta, wife of our lord Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander.
EDCS-12500238.summary: This is an epitaph for Julia Seia Herennia Sallustia Barbia Orbiaana Augusta, wife of Emperor Severus Alexander, likely dating to his reign (AD 222-235).

EDCS-12700160.text_edition: \n VI S coniugi Marci Aureli Comini Cassiani legati Augg(ustorum) pro praetore clarissimi viri Caius Iulius Rogatianus decurio alae Flaviae ex corniculario eius provectus ab eo\n
EDCS-12700160.translation: To VI S, wife of Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a most distinguished man, legate of the emperors pro praetore. Caius Iulius Rogatianus, optio of the Flavian cavalry wing, promoted by him from his cornicularius.
EDCS-12700160.summary: This inscription honors VI S, wife of a prominent imperial legate, and mentions the career advancement of Caius Iulius Rogatianus, a military officer, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-12900010.translation: To the departed spirits. Geminia Ptolemais lived 65 years. May her bones rest well.
EDCS-12900010.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Geminia Ptolemais, who lived 65 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900011.translation: To the departed spirits. Glycera lived 3 years. She rests here.
EDCS-12900011.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Glycera, who lived 3 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900012.translation: Grania Mura lived 40 years. She rests here.
EDCS-12900012.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Grania Mura, who lived 40 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900013.translation: To the departed spirits. Grania Natalis lived 5 years. She rests here. May her bones rest well.

EDCS-12900014.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Grania Nigella lived for 27 years.

EDCS-12900017.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Heraclida lived for 27 years.

EDCS-12900018.translation: Lucius Herennius Fortunatus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived for 60 years and is buried here. May your bones rest well.

EDCS-12900019.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Hilaria lived for 45 years.

EDCS-12900020.translation: In memory of Quintus Hirrius Martialis, dearest son. He lies here. He lived for 2 years.

EDCS-12900021.translation: In memory of Honrata (name uncertain), who lived for 60 years.
EDCS-12900021.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of a woman named Honrata.

EDCS-12900025.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Horatia Monnula of the Quirina tribe (?).
EDCS-12900025.summary: A funerary epitaph for Horatia Monnula, possibly indicating tribal affiliation.

EDCS-12900026.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Hordionius Salutaris lived for 8 years and is buried here.

EDCS-12900027.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Crescenstianus, son of Decimus Iulius, lived for 20 years and is buried here. To the spirits of the departed. To Iulia Honorata, dearest wife, lived for 30 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900027.summary: A double funerary epitaph commemorating both Crescenstianus and his mother, Iulia Honorata.

EDCS-12900028.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Iulius Gemellus lived for 56 years.

EDCS-12900029.translation: To Gaius Julius Honoratius the Younger, his brother, devoted. He lived Z(?) years.

EDCS-12900030.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Julius Victoricus lived 51 years.

EDCS-12900034.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Iotelus Citirua(ri)us lived 81 years. Here he lies. May your bones rest well and happily.
EDCS-12900034.summary: This epitaph is for Titus Iotelus Citirua(ri)us, who lived 81 years and is buried here, with a wish for his repose, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900035.summary: This inscription records the life of Gaius Irius Cate(h)varius, who died at the age of 22, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900039.translation: Lucius Julius, son of Lucius, ASM[---], lived [---] years.

EDCS-12900043.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Crescens lived 12 years.

EDCS-12900044.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Julius Crescenus lived 20 years.

EDCS-12900047.translation: Lucius Julius Episucus lived 11 years. To the departed spirits. Lucius Julius Kandidus lived 80 years. May your bones rest well. To the departed spirits. Sittia Spenis, whoever loves a spouse, let them join this example of love. It is indeed a sweet solace of life to depart for the gods above with our dearest son Episucus. She lived 57 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900047.summary: This inscription contains multiple funerary epitaphs, including one for Lucius Julius Episucus (aged 11), Lucius Julius Kandidus (aged 80), and Sittia Spenis (aged 57), who died with her son Episucus.

EDCS-12900055.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Fortis, who lived 65 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900057.translation: To Gaius Julius Fuscus, son of Gaius, lived 10 years.

EDCS-12900059.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulius Gudulus, who lived 65 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900060.translation: To the shades of the departed. Julius Hospes set this up for his dearest brother, who lived 15 years and is buried here.

EDCS-12900062.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Hospitalis, son of Titus, who lived 16 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900069.translation: Gaius Julius Onesimus lived 75 years. He lies here.

EDCS-12900072.translation: Gaius Julius Pius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 105 years. He lies here.

EDCS-12900081.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the gods of the underworld, recording the age at death of Publius Julius Severianus.

EDCS-12900083.summary: A funerary epitaph, possibly for multiple individuals, dedicated to the gods of the underworld and recording the ages at death of Quintus Julius Istatianus and Quintus Julius Aemilianus.

EDCS-12900086.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulius Victor lived 14 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12900086.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Victor, who lived 14 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900087.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by sons and grandsons for Iulius Victor, who lived 71 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900088.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulius Victorinus lived 100 years.

EDCS-12900091.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Urbanus, who lived 11 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900092.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Iulius Urbanus, freedman of Publius, who lived 70 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900093.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia, daughter of Gaius.

EDCS-12900095.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Antonia lived 11 years.

EDCS-12900096.summary: A tombstone for Iulia Anulla, who lived 50 years, with a wish for her bones to rest well, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900097.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Castula lived 40 years. She is laid here.

EDCS-12900103.translation: Iulia Ecloge, freedwoman of Publius, lived 70 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-12900103.summary: A tombstone for Iulia Ecloge, a freedwoman of Publius, who lived 70 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900105.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Fatalis lived 18 years. She is here.

EDCS-12900111.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Honorata lived 16 years. She lies here.
EDCS-12900111.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Honorata, recording her age and that her burial place is here.

EDCS-12900112.translation: Iulia Hospita lived 60 years; may her bones rest well.

EDCS-12900113.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Ianuaria lived 75 years.

EDCS-12900125.summary: A damaged funerary epitaph for Iulia Maximina, noting a very short life.

EDCS-12900127.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Monnula lived 41 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900127.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Monnula, who died at age 41, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900128.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Mustia, daughter of Caius, lived 31 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900128.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Mustia, daughter of Caius, who died at age 31, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900129.translation: Iulia Nabira lived 50 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900129.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Nabira, who died at age 50, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900131.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Nicerosa lived 33 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900131.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Nicerosa, who died at age 33, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900134.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Nusanula(?) lived 30 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900134.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Nusanula (?), who died at age 30, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900135.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Octavia lived 70 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900135.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Octavia, who died at age 70, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900137.translation: Iulia Renata, most holy of all women, completed her years and followed her husband, Iulius Sarnianus, to the afterlife. She lived 70 years.

EDCS-12900139.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Restuta lived for 13 years.

EDCS-12900140.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Rogata lived for 100 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900140.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Rogata, who died at the age of 100.

EDCS-12900141.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Rogata lived for 35 years and is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-12900141.summary: A tombstone for Iulia Rogata, who died at 35, including a wish for her peaceful rest.

EDCS-12900144.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Silvana lived for 60 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900144.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Silvana, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-12900145.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Speni lived for 11 years and is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-12900145.summary: A tombstone for Iulia Speni, who died at the age of 11, including a wish for her peaceful rest.

EDCS-12900146.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Telesphoris, who was also known as Ingenua, lived for 7 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900146.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Telesphoris (also Ingenua), who died at the age of 7.

EDCS-12900147.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Telesphoris lived for 45 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900147.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Telesphoris, who died at the age of 45.

EDCS-12900148.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Venusta lived for 35 years and is buried here.
EDCS-12900148.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Venusta, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-12900149.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [I]ulia Vicari
EDCS-12900149.summary: An incomplete tombstone inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed for Iulia Vicari.

EDCS-12900150.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Iulia Victoria [Iu]daea(?) [---]
EDCS-12900150.summary: An incomplete tombstone inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed for Iulia Victoria, possibly of Jewish origin.

EDCS-12900152.translation: To the gods, the shades. Iulia Urbica, daughter of Lucius, lived 25 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900152.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iulia Urbica, daughter of Lucius, who lived 25 years.

EDCS-12900153.translation: To the gods, the shades. Quintus Iunius Mamil[, lived 61 years.
EDCS-12900153.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Quintus Iunius Mamil[, who lived 61 years.

EDCS-12900154.translation: To the gods, the shades. Iunia Sittiola lived 18 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900154.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iunia Sittiola, who lived 18 years.

EDCS-12900155.translation: Intileia Faustilla, daughter of Quintus, lived 5 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900155.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Intileia Faustilla, daughter of Quintus, who lived 5 years.

EDCS-12900156.translation: To the gods, the shades. Iuventia Lucilla lived 70 years. She is buried [here].
EDCS-12900156.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Iuventia Lucilla, who lived 70 years.

EDCS-12900157.translation: To the gods, the shades. Iuventia Rogata, a wife for one man, lived 75 years. She is buried here. // To the gods, the shades. Lucius [---] Restitutus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, father, lived 6[3] years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12900157.summary: A tombstone recording the deaths of Iuventia Rogata and her father Lucius Restitutus, who lived 75 and 63 years respectively.

EDCS-12900158.translation: To the gods, the shades. Marcus L. Gemellus lived 70 years.
EDCS-12900158.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Marcus L. Gemellus, who lived 70 years.

EDCS-12900159.translation: Marcus L. Herma lived 65 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12900159.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Marcus L. Herma, who lived 65 years.

EDCS-12900163.translation: For the burial places of the gens Lepidia: Lepidus Nampulus and Stennia Potita and their heirs or their heirs are living in their time. Istantius made [this].
EDCS-12900163.summary: A funerary monument recording the provision for the burial places of the Lepidii family, made by Istantius.

EDCS-12900164.translation: To the gods, the shades. Gaius Licinius Castus lived 101 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12900164.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Gaius Licinius Castus, who lived 101 years.

EDCS-12900165.translation: To the shades below. Publius Licinius Hermes lived 45 years. May you rest well here.
EDCS-12900165.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Licinius Hermes, who lived 45 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900166.translation: Lucius Licinius Perennius, son of Caius, lived 101 years. May his bones rest well here.
EDCS-12900166.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Licinius Perennius, son of Caius, who lived 101 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900167.translation: To the shades below. Marcus Licinius Rufinus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 86 years for his most beloved father. May he rest here.
EDCS-12900167.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Licinius Rufinus, honoring his father, who lived 86 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900168.translation: Caius Licinius Silvanus, with Hanno, lived 19 years. He rests here.
EDCS-12900168.summary: A funerary inscription for Caius Licinius Silvanus, who lived 19 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900169.translation: To the shades below. Licinius Zeius lived 60 years.
EDCS-12900169.summary: A funerary inscription for Licinius Zeius, who lived 60 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900171.translation: To the shades below. Licinia Ingenua lived 45 years. She rests here.
EDCS-12900171.summary: A funerary inscription for Licinia Ingenua, who lived 45 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900172.translation: To the shades below. Livia Tertullina lived 25 years.
EDCS-12900172.summary: A funerary inscription for Livia Tertullina, who lived 25 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900173.translation: To the shades below. Lollius Honoratus lived 80 years.
EDCS-12900173.summary: A funerary inscription for Lollius Honoratus, who lived 80 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900174.translation: To the shades below. Lucius Lollius Rusticus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a 'nurtured one', lived 19 years.
EDCS-12900174.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Lollius Rusticus, son of Lucius, who lived 19 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900175.translation: Marcus Lollius Synecdexius lived 50 years. He rests here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-12900175.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Lollius Synecdexius, who lived 50 years, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900176.translation: Here lies Lucida, a slave girl of Augustus, to her most obedient husband.
EDCS-12900176.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucida, a slave girl of the emperor, set up by her husband.

EDCS-12900177.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucida, a slave girl of our Augustus, lived 11 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-12900177.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucida, a slave girl of the emperor, who died at the age of 11.

EDCS-12900178.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Lucilia Felicia at the age of 80.

EDCS-12900180.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Lutatius Ingenuus lived 2 years (?).
EDCS-12900180.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Lutatius Ingenuus, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-12900181.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Quintus Lutatius Principinus at the age of 90.

EDCS-12900182.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Lurius Faustus at the age of 60.

EDCS-12900183.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius M. O. lived 60 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-12900183.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius M. O., who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-12900184.translation: Lucius M. son of Lucius Urbanus lived 38 years. Buried on the 26th of December.
EDCS-12900184.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Urbanus, who died at the age of 38 and was buried on December 26th.

EDCS-12900185.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aecilius Marcianus lived 41 years.
EDCS-12900185.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Aecilius Marcianus, who died at the age of 41.

EDCS-12900186.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Mecilia Castula, a most pleasant woman, lived 80 years. Quintus Cassius Felix for his most loving, most dutiful, most joyful sister.
EDCS-12900186.summary: This is a tombstone for Mecilia Castula, set up by her brother Quintus Cassius Felix.

EDCS-12900187.translation: Decima Maecilia Hilara lived 80 years; she lies here.
EDCS-12900187.summary: This epitaph records the death of Decima Maecilia Hilara, who lived to be 80 years old.

EDCS-12900188.translation: To the departed spirits. Maecilia Onesime lived 35 years; she lies here.
EDCS-12900188.summary: This epitaph records the death of Maecilia Onesime, who lived to be 35 years old.

EDCS-12900189.translation: To the departed spirits. Manius Marcianus lived 20 years; he lies here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-12900189.summary: This epitaph records the death of Manius Marcianus, who lived to be 20 years old and includes a wish for his repose.

EDCS-12900190.translation: Marcus Magnius Felix, also known as Secretius, lived 40 years.
EDCS-12900190.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Marcus Magnius Felix, also known as Secretius, who lived to be 40 years old.

EDCS-12900204.translation: Aulus Marcius Iucundus, son of Aulus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 14 years. He is buried here. May your bones rest well.

EDCS-12900206.translation: Marcia Tertulla, daughter of Caius, lived 30 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well.

EDCS-12900215.translation: Lucius Memmius Oceanus lived 25 years; he rests here.
EDCS-12900215.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Lucius Memmius Oceanus.

EDCS-12900216.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Meridiana lived 50 years.

EDCS-12900220.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Modia Hospita lived 29 years. Celius Viator lived 5 years; he rests here.

EDCS-12900223.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Munatia Secundilla lived X years [restored].

EDCS-12900224.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Mundicius Saturninus lived 95 years; his bones lie here, may they rest well. To the spirits of the departed. Umbria Matronica, the maturity of humanity, I was a long-serving slave, I appeared to this divinity and this religious practice, to whom I served for eighty years, chaste and modest and insistent even with bare feet, to all the cities of the earth, and for this reason I suffered what I deserved from her, that the earth might receive me kindly. She lived 115 years; her bones lie here, may they rest well. Matronica.
EDCS-12900224.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription for two women, Marcus Mundicius Saturninus and Umbria Matronica, who lived to advanced ages and served a particular cult, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12900225.translation: Munnia Reducta lived 20 years; she lies here.

EDCS-12900309.translation: Gnaeus Roman(i)us Longus, son of Gnaeus, of the tribe Quirina, lived 90 years; he lies here.
