EDCS-24500322.text_edition: \n Aulo Larcio Auli filio Quirina Prisco VIviro equitum Romanorum Xviro stlitibus iudicandis quaestori provinciae Asiae legato Augusti legionis leg III sic Scythicae p<ro=ED> legato consulare sic provinciae Syriae tribuno plebis praetori praefecto frumenti dandi ex senatus consulto legato provinciae Baeticae Hispaniae proconsuli provinciae Galliae Narbonensis legato Augusti legionis II Augustae legato Augusti pro praetore exercitus provinciae Africae VIIviro epulonum consuli designato patrono coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica \n
EDCS-24500322.translation: To Aulus Larcius Priscus, son of Aulus, of the Voltinia tribe, six-member official of the Roman knights, judge in charge of lawsuits, quaestor of the province of Asia, legate of the 3rd Scythica legion, legate with consular authority of the province of Syria, tribune of the plebs, praetor, prefect in charge of distributing grain by decree of the Senate, legate of the province of Baetica in Hispania, proconsul of the province of Gallia Narbonensis, legate of the 2nd Augusta legion, legate with praetorian authority of the army of the province of Africa, seven-member official of the Epulones, designated consul, and patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions and from public funds.
EDCS-24500322.summary: This lengthy inscription lists the extensive career of Aulus Larcius Priscus, a Roman official who served in numerous high-ranking positions, ending with his dedication as patron of the colony by public decree and funds, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-24700788.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XX imperatori III consuli III patri patriae liberalissimoque? principi dedicante Publio Caelio Optato legato Augusti pro praetore |(centuriones) et veterani legionis III Augustae qui militare coeperunt divo Pio III et Marco Aurelio Vero II et Stloga et Severo consulibus\n
EDCS-24700788.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 20th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, and most generous princeps; Publius Caelius Optatus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, dedicated this, along with the centurions and veterans of the 3rd Augusta legion who began their service under the deified Pius and Marcus Aurelius Verus (second consulship) and Stloga and Severus.
EDCS-24700788.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus by Publius Caelius Optatus, along with centurions and veterans of the 3rd Augusta legion, likely dating to the period when Marcus Aurelius was consul for the third time (AD 170s).

EDCS-24700801.text_edition: \n Publio Novio Saturnino Martiali Marcello Luci Novi Crispini legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati filio speculatores\n
EDCS-24700801.translation: To Publius Novius Saturninus Martialis Marcellus, son of Lucius Novius Crispinus, imperial legate, consul-designate, by the speculatores.
EDCS-24700801.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Publius Novius Saturninus Martialis Marcellus, son of an imperial legate and consul-designate, made by the speculatores.

EDCS-24700812.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti tribunicia potestate XVI consuli III primi ordines et centuriones et evocatus legionis III Augustae dedicante Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato\n
EDCS-24700812.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of Nerva, in his sixteenth year of tribunician power, consul for the third time, by the first cohort, the centurions, and the evocati of the Third Augustan Legion, dedicated by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with the rank of proconsul, consul-designate.
EDCS-24700812.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus by the soldiers of the Third Augustan Legion and their legate Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, during his sixteenth tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-24800676.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino legato Augusti pro praetore Caius Antonius Cai filius Collina Alexander Antiochia curiae Hadrianae felici veteranorum legionis III Augustae ob honorem flaminatus perpetui quod in se absentem contulerunt promissa statua ex HS IIII milibus nummum ampliata pecunia fecit\n
EDCS-24800676.translation: Sacred to Fortuna Augusta; Lucius Novius Crispinus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, dedicated this, and Caius Antonius Alexander, son of Caius, of the Collina tribe, from Antioch, belonging to the Hadrianic curia, a fortunate veteran of the 3rd Augusta legion, for the honor of perpetual flaminate, which they bestowed upon him in his absence, with funds increased from 4,000 sesterces, he erected the promised statue.
EDCS-24800676.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Fortuna Augusta by a veteran of the 3rd Augusta legion, Caius Antonius Alexander, honoring his appointment to perpetual flaminate, made possible by the legate L. Novius Crispinus and funded with an additional sum, likely in the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24800693.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae sacrum dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino legato Augusti pro praetore Quintus Iulius Quinti filius Quirina Martialis Cirta curiae Hadrianae felici veteranorum legionis III Augustae ob honorem flaminatus perpetui quem in se absentem contulerunt ex HS IIII milibus nummum ampliata pecunia praeter legitimam summam posuit\n
EDCS-24800693.translation: Sacred to Minerva Augusta; Lucius Novius Crispinus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, dedicated this, and Quintus Julius Martialis, son of Quintus, of the Voltinia tribe, from Cirta, belonging to the Hadrianic curia, a fortunate veteran of the 3rd Augusta legion, for the honor of perpetual flaminate, which they bestowed upon him in his absence, he erected it with funds increased beyond the legitimate sum from 4,000 sesterces.
EDCS-24800693.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Minerva Augusta by a veteran of the 3rd Augusta legion, Quintus Julius Martialis, honoring his appointment to perpetual flaminate, made possible by the legate L. Novius Crispinus and funded with an additional sum, likely in the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24800717.translation: Constantine, we see your Augusti always angry at evils, and we obtain peace. For in this Genius the province shows itself; for he places crosses and terrible battles, the tyrant.
EDCS-24800717.summary: This inscription, likely from the 4th century AD, reflects a turbulent period, possibly referencing Constantine the Great and his struggles against paganism or internal enemies, with the province acknowledging both suffering and eventual peace.

EDCS-24800724.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore claro et inlustri viro \n
EDCS-24800724.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, Imperial Legate, Proconsul, a clear and illustrious man.
EDCS-24800724.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a provincial governor, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24900583.text_edition: \n ------] aequites sic alae Flaviae Severianae VC Gorcianus sic Cluvitus Modius Rusticus tius Lucius Geminius Auanus Aurelius Ianuarius Albino et Maximo consulibus\n
EDCS-24900583.translation: The equites of the Ala Flavia Severiana, Gorcianus, Cluvitus Modius Rusticus, Lucius Geminius Auanus Aurelius Ianuarius, in the consulship of Albinus and Maximus.
EDCS-24900583.summary: This inscription lists members of the Ala Flavia Severiana cavalry regiment, with a date indicated by the consuls Albinus and Maximus (likely 3rd century CE).

EDCS-25001001.text_edition: \n Quintus Pompeius Cottinus beneficiarius vixit annis XXXXVIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-25001001.translation: Quintus Pompeius Cottinus, a beneficiarius, lived 48 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25001001.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Pompeius Cottinus, who served as a beneficiarius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001002.text_edition: \n Memoriae Aureliae Salviae filiae piissimae quae vixit annis III mensibus X diebus VI Salvius pater\n
EDCS-25001002.translation: To the memory of Aurelia Salvia, most dutiful daughter, who lived 3 years, 10 months, and 6 days. Salvius, the father.
EDCS-25001002.summary: This is a memorial epitaph for a young girl, Aurelia Salvia, erected by her father, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Veneriae filiae dulcissimae Hilaritas mater eius vixit annis XXI mensibus XVI\n
EDCS-25001003.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Hilaritas, her mother, [erected this] for her most sweet daughter Veneria, who lived 21 years and 16 months.
EDCS-25001003.summary: This is an epitaph for Veneria, erected by her mother Hilaritas, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Magniae Naidi feminae rarissimae uxori karissimae castissimae piissimae annos XLVIII menses VIII functae Campius dulcissimae maritus\n
EDCS-25001004.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Magnia Naidis, a most rare woman, his dearest and most chaste and most dutiful wife, who lived 48 years and 8 months. Campius, her husband.
EDCS-25001004.summary: This is an epitaph for Magnia Naidis, erected by her husband Campius, celebrating her virtues and likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001005.text_edition: \n Lucius Longeius Primus [] vixit annos LXXXX hic situs est ipse sibi\n
EDCS-25001005.translation: Lucius Longeius Primus lived 90 years; he is buried here. For himself.
EDCS-25001005.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Longeius Primus, who lived to an advanced age and prepared his own burial, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001006.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Italus vixit nnos XXIII Strato vixit nnos XXXXV domini servis bene merentibus fecerunt\n
EDCS-25001006.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Italus lived 23 years. Strato lived 35 years. [Erected by] their masters for well-deserving slaves.
EDCS-25001006.summary: This inscription records the ages of two slaves, Italus and Strato, and states that it was erected by their masters, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001008.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Peticius Datus beneficiarius obita morte vixit annos XXXV hic situs\n
EDCS-25001008.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Peticius Datus, a beneficiarius, died having lived 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25001008.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Peticius Datus, who served as a beneficiarius and died at a young age, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001009.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Tertia vixit annis LXII filius matri carissimae de suo posuit et dedicavit\n
EDCS-25001009.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Julia Tertia lived 62 years. Her son placed and dedicated [this] for his dearest mother at his own expense.
EDCS-25001009.summary: This is an epitaph for Julia Tertia, erected by her son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Liciniae Geminae uxori vixit annos XLV Publius S T maritus fecit\n
EDCS-25001010.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Licinia Gemina, my wife, who lived 45 years. Publius S T, her husband, made this.
EDCS-25001010.summary: A funerary epitaph for Licinia Gemina, dedicated by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Caecilius Castrensis se vivo fecit\n
EDCS-25001011.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caecilius Castrensis made this while alive.
EDCS-25001011.summary: A funerary epitaph where the deceased, Caecilius Castrensis, dedicated his own tomb during his lifetime, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001013.summary: A dedication to the goddess Victoria by Arborius, possibly a father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25001014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Exuperans vixit annos LXV menses VI sepulchrum Iulia Extricata marito dulcissimo fecit cum vixit [vi?]xit et semper omnes aluit\n
EDCS-25001014.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aurelius Exuperans, who lived 65 years and 6 months. Iulia Extricata made this tomb for her dearest husband, who lived and always supported everyone.
EDCS-25001014.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Exuperans, dedicated by his wife Iulia Extricata, and mentioning his generous nature, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25002174.text_edition: \n Cereri matri et filiae Augustis sacrum Caius Bottius Luci filius Quirina Saturninus Abdus Maior aram cum altaribus votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-25002174.translation: Sacred to Ceres, mother and daughters Augustae. Caius Bottius Saturninus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, and Abdus Maior, paid their vow willingly with this altar and surrounding stones.
EDCS-25002174.summary: A dedication of an altar and other stones to Ceres and her divine daughters by Caius Bottius Saturninus and Abdus Maior, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25500161.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. In memory of Clodius, of legal knowledge, most constant. [---] XI
EDCS-25500161.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph commemorating Clodius, described as a knowledgeable and constant jurist, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25500172.translation: Annaedia, who lived 10 years. Ianuaria [made this].
EDCS-25500172.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Annaedia, who died at 10 years old, likely dedicated by Ianuaria, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25600185.text_edition: \n Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Aviola consulibus Decimus N Victor magister et Messius Picatinu<s=I> collega pridie Kalendas Apriles \n
EDCS-25600185.translation: During the consulship of our Emperor lord Gordian Augustus and Aviola, on the day before the Kalends of April, Decimus N Victor, the master, and Messius Picatinus, his colleague.
EDCS-25600185.summary: A record of consuls and officials, Decimus N Victor and Messius Picatinus, for the year of Emperor Gordian Augustus, dating to AD 243.

EDCS-25600400.text_edition: \n Lucio Apronio Pio legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli designato patrono coloniarum ob insignem erga cives benivolentiam et iustitiam eius res publica Siguitanorum\n
EDCS-25600400.translation: To Lucius Apronius Pius, clarissimus vir, legate of Augustus pro praetore, designated consul, patron of the colonies, for his outstanding benevolence and justice towards the citizens, the public administration of the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600400.summary: A dedication by the public administration of the Siguitani to Lucius Apronius Pius, a high-ranking official and patron, praising his justice and benevolence, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25600401.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum agri divisi Siguitanis\n
EDCS-25600401.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus, the public lands of the Siguitani were divided among the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600401.summary: A record of the division of public lands belonging to the Siguitani, authorized by the legate Publius Cassius Secundus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25600402.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum agri accepti Cirtensium\n
EDCS-25600402.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus, of the public land of the Siguitani, land accepted from the Cirtaenses.
EDCS-25600402.summary: This inscription records an administrative decision regarding land boundaries, issued by the legate of Augustus, Publius Cassius Secundus, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25600403.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti agri publici Siguitanorum agri divisi Siguitanis\n
EDCS-25600403.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of Augustus, of the public land of the Siguitani, land divided among the Siguitani.
EDCS-25600403.summary: This inscription records an administrative decision concerning the division of public land among the Siguitani, issued by the legate of Augustus, Publius Cassius Secundus, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25600621.text_edition: \n Res publica Verecundensium dedicante Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato\n
EDCS-25600621.translation: The community of Verecundenses dedicates, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legate of Augustus pro praetore and consul designate.
EDCS-25600621.summary: This inscription records the dedication of something by the community of Verecundenses, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus acting as legate of Augustus pro praetore and consul designate, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25600722.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Decimus Calvinius...
EDCS-25600722.summary: This is the beginning of a funerary inscription (dis manibus) for Decimus Calvinius, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-25601741.translation: Gaius Julius Severus, son of Gaius, of the Papiria tribe, a pious man, lived 13 years. Julius Valens, optio, made this for his brother.
EDCS-25601741.summary: This is a funerary epigram for Gaius Julius Severus, who died at the age of 13, with the inscription erected by his brother, Julius Valens.

EDCS-25601742.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Iullius Victor lived 11 years, 6 and a half hours. His parents made this.
EDCS-25601742.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Iullius Victor, who died at the age of 11 years and 6.5 hours, erected by his parents.

EDCS-25800014.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Nervae filio Nerva Traiano Augusto Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IV consule III patre patriae Lucio Munatio Gallo legato Augusti pro praetore XIX\n
EDCS-25800014.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, with Lucius Munatius Gallus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, the 19th [year implied].
EDCS-25800014.summary: This inscription records a date within the reign of Emperor Trajan, referencing his titles and Lucius Munatius Gallus as legate of Augustus pro praetore, likely concerning a public work or decree.

EDCS-25800017.translation: Under the Emperors Caesars Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the country for the 2nd time, consul, proconsul, and Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Voldumianus Volussianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, proconsul, the community of the colony of Thamugadi [records].
EDCS-25800017.summary: This inscription records a decree or event concerning the colony of Thamugadi, under the joint rule of Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volussianus, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-25800075.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, with Lucius Munatius Gallus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, the 17th [year implied].
EDCS-25800075.summary: This inscription records a date within the reign of Emperor Trajan, referencing his titles and Lucius Munatius Gallus as legate of Augustus pro praetore, likely concerning a public work or decree.

EDCS-25900013.text_edition: \n Caio Velleio Paterculo legato Augusti legionis III Augustae XXIX\n
EDCS-25900013.translation: To Gaius Velleius Paterculus, legate of Augustus of the Legio III Augusta, the 29th [year implied].
EDCS-25900013.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Velleius Paterculus, legate of the Legio III Augusta, likely recording a military achievement or administrative act, possibly in the early 1st century CE.

EDCS-25900026.text_edition: \n via facta a Larcio Prisco legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-25900026.translation: Road made by Larcio Priscus, legate of the emperor with the power of a propraetor.
EDCS-25900026.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road by Larcio Priscus, a legatus Augusti pro praetore, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-25900482.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patre patriae IIII et Marco Aurelio Caesare II consulibus per Prastinam Messalinum legatum Augusti pro praetore vexillatio legionis VI ferratae viam fecit\n
EDCS-25900482.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, for the fourth time, and Marcus Aurelius Caesar for the second time, Prastina Messalinus, legate of the emperor with the power of a propraetor, constructed the road by a detachment of the Sixth Legion Ferrata.
EDCS-25900482.summary: This inscription documents the construction of a road by a vexillation of the Legio VI Ferrata, supervised by the legate Prastina Messalinus, during the joint consulship of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius, dating to 140-144 AD.

EDCS-25900488.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto patre patriae tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum LVIIII\n
EDCS-25900488.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power, for the second time, Lucius Naevius Quadratus, legate of the emperor with the power of a propraetor, [marked] 59 miles from Lambaesis.
EDCS-25900488.summary: This inscription, made under the governorship of Lucius Naevius Quadratus during Pertinax's consulship, records a milestone indicating distance from Lambaesis, dating to 193 AD.

EDCS-25900492.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto pro praetore tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum L\n
EDCS-25900492.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, with the power of a propraetor, holding tribunician power, for the second time, Lucius Naevius Quadratus, legate of the emperor with the power of a propraetor, [marked] 50 miles from Lambaesis.
EDCS-25900492.summary: This inscription, similar to the previous one and also made under Lucius Naevius Quadratus during Pertinax's consulship, records a milestone indicating distance from Lambaesis, dating to 193 AD.

EDCS-25900545.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti pontes viae novae Rusicadensis res publica Cirtensium sua pecunia fecit Sexto Iulio Maiore legato Augusti legionis III Augustae pro praetore\n
EDCS-25900545.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, the public works of the new Rusicadae roads were funded by the state of the Cirta people, with Sextus Julius Maior, legate of the Third Augusta Legion with the power of a propraetor, overseeing.
EDCS-25900545.summary: This inscription records the construction of new roads by the city of Cirta, funded by the state and supervised by Sextus Julius Maior, legate of Legio III Augusta, under the authority of Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-26000980.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus and Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Voludumianus Volusianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, consuls.
EDCS-26000980.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, likely dating to their joint consulship around 251-253 AD.

EDCS-26100133.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and to Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veledumianus Volussianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the fatherland, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, to you unconquered Emperors and your [families/dependents].
EDCS-26100133.summary: This inscription honors Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus with their full titles, likely dating to their consulship and reign, circa 251-253 AD.

EDCS-26100328.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Cominius Paliarius vixit annos LXXXI\n
EDCS-26100328.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Cominius Paliarius lived 81 years.
EDCS-26100328.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death and age of Lucius Cominius Paliarius, a funerary dedication to the Di Manes.

EDCS-26100341.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Clodius Urbanus vixit annos LXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-26100341.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Clodius Urbanus lived 75 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-26100341.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death and age of Marcus Clodius Urbanus, indicating his burial place, a dedication to the Di Manes.

EDCS-26100343.text_edition: \n Lucius Cominius Quetus vixit annos LXXXXII\n
EDCS-26100343.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death and age of Lucius Cominius Quetus.

EDCS-27300292.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani nepote divi Nervae pronepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio consule IIII tribuniciae potestatis XXI Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus patronus coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-27300292.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, in his fourth consulship, holding tribunician power for the 21st time, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, imperial legate with praetorian powers, consul-designate, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-27300292.summary: This inscription records a public building project funded by the colony and supervised by its patron, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-27300294.text_edition: \n et onerari se inlicitis --- militum atque officialium exactionibus decreti concili quod suscitavitquerelas cum magno animi mei dolore audivi temporum illorum quorum fuit ad nunc quis aequo animo ferat exactionibus inlicitis quibus imponunt fortunis alienis imminere ruinam m exauriant compendis suis uam populi vel fisci debiti I procere quis non et milites parentium ac liberorum summa excipit officiales munificentia ne quasi quodam more constituto publici vectigalis paterentur ne posthac admittant aut poenae iis prodelicti qualitate inrogentur s de qua re et proccuratoribus meis litteras misi et rescriptum meum etiam provincialibus innotescere volui ciant Lucius Apronius Pius legatus Augusti [] IIIA eius circa provinciam suam hic \n
EDCS-27300294.translation: And to be burdened by illegal exactions of soldiers and officials, I heard with great grief of spirit the decree of the council which stirred up complaints. Who in those times could bear with an even mind the illegal exactions, by which they threaten ruin to others' fortunes and enrich themselves, while the people's or the treasury's debt is diminished. Who would not have received the sums for parents and children, lest officials, as if by established custom, suffer the public revenue to be diminished? Lest they be admitted henceforth, or punishments be inflicted upon them according to the nature of their offense. Concerning which matter I have also sent a letter to my procurators, and I wished my rescript to be made known also to the provincials, let Lucius Apronius Pius, imperial legate [IIIA] around his province here.
EDCS-27300294.summary: This inscription is part of a rescript by an imperial legate, Lucius Apronius Pius, addressing illegal financial exactions by soldiers and officials, aiming to protect the populace and public funds.

EDCS-27600035.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britain, conqueror of Germany, emperor for the third time, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, restored [this monument].
EDCS-27600035.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius (Caracalla), dating to the period of his 19th tribunician power.

EDCS-27600254.translation: To our Lord Flavius Crispinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-27600254.summary: This is a dedication to Flavius Crispinus, a Caesar, likely adorning a building or monument.

EDCS-27600278.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-27600278.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, a common imperial titulature for the period.

EDCS-27600282.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-27600282.summary: This inscription records the reign of Emperor Maximian, likely as Augustus, during a period when he was co-emperor.

EDCS-27600283.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Maximian, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-27600283.summary: This inscription records the reign of Emperor Maximian, likely as Augustus, during a period when he was co-emperor.

EDCS-27600327.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus, consul for the second time.
EDCS-27600327.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Diocletian during his second consulship, likely marking a public work or honor.

EDCS-27900240.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator IV consul V pater patriae Lucio Minicio Natale legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-27900240.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the ninth time, emperor for the fourth time, consul for the fifth time, father of the country, to Lucius Minicius Natalis, imperial legate with praetorian powers.
EDCS-27900240.summary: This inscription records an honor or dedication to Lucius Minicius Natalis, legate of Emperor Trajan, during Trajan's ninth tribunician power and fifth consulship.

EDCS-28000969.text_edition: \n Bombia Sexti filia Martila vixit annos XXXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-28000969.translation: Bombia Martila, daughter of Sextus, lived 40 years; she lies here.
EDCS-28000969.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, parentage, age, and burial place of a woman named Bombia Martila.

EDCS-28000970.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Brinnia Iucunda vixit annos CXXXV hic sita <e=L>(st) \n
EDCS-28000970.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Brinnia Iucunda lived for 135 years and is buried here.
EDCS-28000970.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Brinnia Iucunda, who lived to an exceptional age.

EDCS-28000971.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Horatia Senecionis filia Monnula vixit annos L hic sita est\n
EDCS-28000971.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Monnula, daughter of Horatia Senecio, lived for 50 years and is buried here.
EDCS-28000971.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Monnula, identified by her mother's name.

EDCS-28000997.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph, likely a tombstone, recording the burial of individuals named Sentius and [?]liavus.

EDCS-28200022.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Arabicus ADIABENICUS MAXIMUS, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Geta Caesar Augustus, our lords, sons, [dedicated this] from Tigava, 10 miles.
EDCS-28200022.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely commemorating a road or milestone construction, indicating the area of Tigava.

EDCS-28200099.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Invictus Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-28200099.summary: This inscription lists the titles and offices held by Emperor Diocletian, likely from an official monument.

EDCS-28200132.translation: To our eternal lord, the everlasting Emperor Constantius, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, the state of the colony of Lambaesis [dedicated this].
EDCS-28200132.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the municipality of Lambaesis to Emperor Constantius, highlighting his divine and eternal status.

EDCS-31700011.text_edition: \n Victoriae nobilissimorum principum nostrorum Valeriani et Gallieni et Valeriani Caesarum Augggustorum Lucius Magius Valerianus vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore aram vovit curante Faustino praefecto alae Pannoniorum\n
EDCS-31700011.translation: To the Victory of our most noble emperors Valerian and Gallienus and Valerian Caesar, Augusti, Lucius Magius Valerianus, most distinguished man, legate of the Augusti pro praetore, dedicated this altar, under the care of Faustinus, prefect of the ala Pannoniorum.
EDCS-31700011.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to the Victory of the emperors Valerian and Gallienus, by Lucius Magius Valerianus, a provincial governor, with the involvement of Faustinus, a prefect of an auxiliary cavalry unit.

EDCS-31700019.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate X imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino legato Augusti pro praetore Titus Flavius Titi filius Tromentina Firmus Salona primus pilus legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-31700019.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, emperor for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, by Lucius Novius Crispinus, legate of Augustus pro praetore, Titus Flavius Firmus, son of Titus, of the Tromentina tribe, primus pilus of the III Augusta legion, dedicated.
EDCS-31700019.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Titus Flavius Firmus, a distinguished officer (primus pilus), to Emperor Antoninus Pius, detailing his titles and the dedication by a legate.

EDCS-38301833.text_edition: \n Domino orbis Invicto ac perpetuo principi domino nostro Flavio Iulio Constantio victori semper Augusto Flavius Ovidius Apthonius vir clarissimus consularis\n
EDCS-38301833.translation: To our lord, unconquered and eternal prince, our lord Flavius Julius Constantius, always victorious Augustus, Flavius Ovidius Apthonius, most distinguished man, consular.
EDCS-38301833.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantius by Flavius Ovidius Apthonius, who held a high administrative rank (consularis).

EDCS-43600026.translation: Up to this point. Aurelius Marcinus, a soldier of the province of Moesia Superior, missed in battle, lived for 35 years, served for 14 years. Aurelius Victor [and] Flavius Severianus set [this up] for a well-deserving man.
EDCS-43600026.summary: This funerary monument commemorates Aurelius Marcinus, a soldier who died in battle after 14 years of service, and was set up by his comrades Aurelius Victor and Flavius Severianus.

EDCS-43700621.text_edition: \n [Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius] [Commodus Antoninus] [Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus] [pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate ] [imperator consul pater patriae divi] Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Marco Valerio Maximiano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro VIII\n
EDCS-43700621.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, emperor, consul, father of the country, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, dedicated by Marcus Valerius Maximianus, clarissimus vir, governor with the power of a propraetor, for the eighth time.
EDCS-43700621.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Commodus, likely part of a larger monument, and records him as having the title of governor with the power of a propraetor eight times.

EDCS-43700685.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, for the fourth time consul.
EDCS-43700685.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely inscribed on a public building or monument, noting his fourth consulship.

EDCS-44300051.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Traiano Hadriano Augusto fortissimo liberalissimoque le[gio III Augusta] adprobato exercitu Imperator Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus legionem suam III Augustam exercitationibus inspectis adlocutus est iis quae infra scripta sunt Torquato II et Libone consulibus Kalendis Iuliis at pedites etis pro causa vestra legatus meus quae dicenda vobis aput sic me fuissent omnia mihi pro vobis ipse dixit quod cohors abest quod omnibus annis per vices in officium proconsulis mittitur quod ante annum tertium cohortem et quaternos ex centuriis in supplementum comparum tertianorum dedistis quod multae quod diversae stationes vos distinent quod nostra memoria bis non tantum mutastis castra sed et nova fecistis ob haec excusatos vos haberem si legio diu exercitatione cessasset sed nihil aut cessavistis vobis excusationeretis va RIVMILRVXIII ILIII IL VIDE sse vobis primi ordines et centuriones agiles more suo fuerunt equitibus legionis exercitationes militares quodam modo suas leges habent quibus si quit adiciatur aut detrahatur aut minor exercitatio fit aut difficilior quantum autem difficultatis additur tantum gratiae demitur vos ex difficilibus difficillimum fecistis ut loricati iaculationem perageretis O quin immo et animum probo EIIII PILI EII tationis ALII plures dies divisas uno die peregistis murum longi operis et qualis mansuris hibernaculis fieri solet non multo diutius exstrucxistis sic quam caespite exstruitur qui modulo pari caesus et vehitur facile et tractatur et sine molestia struitur ut mollis et planus pro natura sua vos lapidibus grandibus gravibus inaequalibus quos neque vehere neque attollere neque locare quis possit nisi ut inaequalitates inter se conpareant sic fossam glaria duram scabramque recte percussistis et radendo levem reddidistis opere probato introgressi castra raptim et cibum et arma cepistis equitem emissum secuti magno clamore revertentem per III Idus Iulias ala I Pannoniorum omnia per ordinem egistis campum incursionibus complestis iaculati estis non ineleganter hastis brevibus et duris lanceas plures vestrum permiserunt valuistis et hic agiliter et heri velociter si quit sic defuisset desiderarem si quit sic eminuisset designarem tota exercitatione peraeque placuistis Catullinus legatus meus clarissimus in omnibus quibus praeest parem curam suam exhibet praefectus vester sollicite videtur vobis attendere congiarium accipite viator iam in Commagenorum campos Allet equitibus cohortis VI Commagenorum difficile est cohortales equites etiam per se placere difficilius post alarem exercitationem non displicere alia spatia campi alius iaculantium numerus frequens dextrator cantabricus densus equorum forma armorum cultus pro stipendii modo verum vos fastidium calore vitastis strenue faciendo quae fieri debebant addidistis ut et lapides fundis mitteretis et missilibus confligeretis saluistis ubique expedite Catullini legati mei clarissimi viri apparet quod tales vos sub IIOI I Iuliis cohorti II Ham(iorum) ra vobis refragata S us in campo iusto S VIS ipsis in laudo quod CONV vos ad hanc exercitationem dimicationis imaginem accepit et sic exercet laudare vos possim Cornelianus praefectus vester satisfecit contrari discursus non placent mihi nec divo patri qui meus est auctor e tecto transcurrat eques et PEnisi videt qua vadat aut si voluerit ecum R quin sit obnoxius caliculis tectis tis congredi debetis concurrere iam adversus hosti facienda Nonis Iuliis Zarai cohorti I Flaviae? equitatae? ISIIretis et DI fecistis et manibus non languidis idnon ad signum miseritis quod iam hostis M vos mittendi saepius et instantius stis ultra scamna non audeat castra tarde iunxistis T erumpetis VDD si erat E omnium ordinume multa fecistis clarissimi viri excepistis tis et summ et ex equis per tot am quoque celeriter exercitatione C iaculari non potuit qu O celer hastatus rect os exercuit rectam IS iuvat ita C \n
EDCS-44300051.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, most brave and most generous. Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus inspected the exercises of his Third Augustan Legion and addressed them with the following words, when Torquatus and Libo were consuls, on the Kalends of July. To the infantry: My legate has told me all that needed to be said to you by me, as if I myself had said it to you, concerning why the cohort is absent, why every year in rotation it is sent to the proconsul's office, why three years ago you provided a cohort and four men from each century as reinforcements for the 'tertianarii', why many and diverse assignments keep you busy, why within our memory you have not only changed camps twice but also built new ones. For these reasons I would excuse you if the legion had long ceased its exercises, but you have not ceased. If you were to excuse yourselves, the first ranks and centurions would have been agile in their manner. The military exercises of the legionary cavalry have their own laws, as it were. If anything is added or subtracted from them, either the exercise becomes less demanding or more difficult. However much difficulty is added, that much praise is diminished. You have made the most difficult task even more difficult, so that you could perform the javelin throw in armor. Indeed, I approve of your spirit. You have completed in one day exercises that were divided over many days. You constructed a long wall, such as is usually built for permanent winter quarters, not much longer ago. As a wall built from turf, which is cut to the same size, is easily moved and handled and built without difficulty, being soft and flat by its nature, you have rightly struck and smoothed a hard, rough gravel ditch. With the work approved, you entered the camp hastily, took both food and weapons, and pursued the cavalry that had been released. On the Ides of July, the First Wing of Pannonians, you performed all actions in order, filled the field with your incursions, and threw javelins not inartistically with short and hard spears. Many of you allowed lances. You performed well here, and yesterday, swiftly. If anything had been lacking, I would mention it; if anything had stood out, I would point it out. You have pleased equally throughout the entire exercise. My most distinguished legate Catullinus shows the same care in all that he is in charge of. Your prefect seems to attend to you diligently. Accept your donative, traveler. Now, in the fields of the Commageni, the six cohorts of Commagenian cavalry are being sent. It is difficult for cohort cavalrymen to please even on their own, and more difficult not to displease after the exercise of the ala. Different areas of the field, a different number of frequent javelin throwers, the dense Cantabrian right flank, the appearance of the horses, the condition of the armor according to the amount of pay. But you have avoided weariness by the heat by strenuously doing what had to be done, and you have added to it, so that you have also thrown stones with slings and engaged in missile combat. You have all succeeded everywhere with ease. It is clear from my most distinguished legate Catullinus that such men are under his command. The Second Cohort of Hamii... you did not resist... in the proper field... I myself praise you because the legion accepted you for this exercise, a representation of combat, and thus trains you. I can praise you. Your prefect Cornelius has satisfied me. Conflicting maneuvers do not please me, nor my deified father, who is my author. A horseman running from under a roof... and sees where he is going... or if he wishes to turn his horse... should engage with covered shields. You must fight, clash! Now, facing the enemy, what must be done. On the Nones of July, the First Wing of Flavians... you did and with no weak hands, you did not send it to the signal, which the enemy would no longer dare to send against you... you are now more often and more urgently... beyond the benches you do not dare... you joined the camp late. You will break out... you did many things, all the ranks, distinguished men, you received... and the javelin from horses... throughout the whole day also quickly, the exercise... could not throw. O how quickly the spearmen exercised straight, straight... it is pleasing... indeed.
EDCS-44300051.summary: This is a lengthy address by Emperor Hadrian to his Third Augustan Legion, praising their discipline, training, and construction efforts, and exhorting them to maintain their high standards, likely inscribed on a military standard or monument.

EDCS-45600002.text_edition: \n Deo patrio praesenti numini Iovi BazosenoRISO Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum frequenter praesentem maiestatem eius expertus sum et dedicavit\n
EDCS-45600002.translation: To the native, present divine power of Jupiter Bazosenus, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, having frequently experienced his present majesty from the rank of principal soldier, dedicated it.
EDCS-45600002.summary: This is a dedication of a shrine to Jupiter Bazosenus by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, the governor of Numidia, who had previously served as a soldier.

EDCS-45600003.text_edition: \n Magniae Urbicae Augustae coniugi domini nostri Marci Aureli Carini Augusti matri castrorum ordo splendidissimus coloniae Lambaesitanorum devotus numminibus eorum dedicante Marco Aurelio Decimo viro perfectissimo praeside provinciae Numidiae\n
EDCS-45600003.translation: To Magnia Urbica Augusta, wife of our lord Marcus Aurelius Carinus Augustus, mother of the camp, the most splendid order of the colony of Lambaesis, devoted to their divine powers, dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia.
EDCS-45600003.summary: This is a dedication by the town council of Lambaesis to Magnia Urbica, wife and mother of emperors Carinus and Numerian, with the governor of Numidia, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, officiating.

EDCS-46400007.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, and Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Numerian Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, Germanicus Maximus, Gothicus Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consuls, fathers of the country, proconsuls, from Tacapas, 39 miles.
EDCS-46400007.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating the distance of 39 miles from Tacapas during the joint consulship of Emperors Carus, Carinus, and Numerian.

EDCS-46700180.translation: To Liber Pater Augustus, from the will of Gaius Julius Victorinus, son of Gaius Julius (Papiria tribe), quaestor, aedile, duovir, perpetual flamen; his sons Julius Scaeva Crescentianus and Victorinus placed and dedicated it. Dedicated on the 16th day before the Kalends of April, in the 187th year of the province.
EDCS-46700180.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Liber Pater made from a will, by the sons of Gaius Julius Victorinus, a prominent local magistrate, and records the provincial year 187.

EDCS-46800201.translation: To our lord Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, most noble Caesar Augustus, son of Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Augustus, our lord, from Calceus, 3 miles.
EDCS-46800201.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating a distance of 3 miles from Calceus, dedicated to the Caesar Maximinus, son of Emperor Maximinus Thrax.

EDCS-47501091.translation: Aelius Felix and Aelia Felicianna, his wife, and their daughters placed and dedicated a table on the 15th day before the Kalends of May, in the province in the year 260.
EDCS-47501091.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a table by Aelius Felix, his wife, and daughters, with the date given as the 15th day before the Kalends of May in provincial year 260.

EDCS-47600546.text_edition: \n Neptuno sacrum Publius Cassius Sophron libertus et nomenclator Publi Cassi Secundi legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati votum solvit\n
EDCS-47600546.translation: Sacred to Neptune. Publius Cassius Sophron, freedman and nomenclator of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of the emperor with the power of a propraetor, and designated consul, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-47600546.summary: This is a votive inscription to Neptune by Publius Cassius Sophron, a freedman and associate of a high-ranking imperial official, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-47600548.text_edition: \n Neptuno Augusto sacrum Bacchylus Titi Caeserni Statiani legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati actor votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-47600548.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Bacchylus, procurator of Titus Caesernius Statianus, governor with proconsular authority, having the consulship designated, gladly and willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-47600548.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to Neptune Augustus by Bacchylus, an official of a Roman governor, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-47700002.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Lucida, daughter of Lucius, pious, lived seven years. Here she lies. First fidelity of her family, most known to all, whose grace and beauty and upright character could not be surpassed by any, content with one husband, alas, what a fate! Yet she lived her years cheerfully with her dear husband, Iabus Arne(n)sius. Furius Iariega laid her bones in the tomb.
EDCS-47700002.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudia Lucida, a young girl who died at age seven, recording her virtues and mentioning her father and husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-54800033.translation: Julius Maximus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, Antoninian, made a cupola for himself during his lifetime at his own expense. Age about 54.
EDCS-54800033.summary: This epitaph records the self-funded construction of a funerary monument (cupola) by Julius Maximus, a soldier of Legio III Augusta, who died around the age of 54, likely from the Severan period (early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-58200007.translation: During the consulship of Emperors Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, for the second time, and Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus. 11 miles.
EDCS-58200007.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 11 miles during the consulship of Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, dating it to 251-253 AD.

EDCS-59600010.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI imperatori V consuli III patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatori V consuli III patri patriae divi Antonini fili divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti [Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus legatus Augustorum pro praetore ] dedicavit\n
EDCS-59600010.translation: Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, legate of the Augusti with proconsular authority, dedicated [this].
EDCS-59600010.summary: This inscription records the dedication by Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, a legate of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, likely dating to the period of their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-59600016.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco Medico Parthico maximo imperatore V pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXIII consule III patre patriae divi Antonini Pii filio divi Hadriani nepote divi Traiani Parthici pronepote divi Nervae abnepote plateam Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-59600016.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Parthicus Maximus, emperor for the fifth time, chief priest, with tribunician power for the twenty-third time, consul for the third time, father of the country, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva. Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, legate of Augustus, governor-designate, patron of the colony, dedicated the street by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-59600016.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a street by Marcus Lucceius Torquatus Bassianus, legate and patron of the colony, under Emperor Marcus Aurelius, dating it to 250 AD.

EDCS-61800279.translation: The council of the colony of Lambesitae, devoted to his divine majesty, dedicated [this] to the most pious and victorious Marcus Aurelius Valerius [[Maximian(?)]] Invictus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-61800279.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Maximian by the colonial council of Lambesis, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-63400138.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-63400138.text_edition: \n ------ pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae] totiusque domus divinae aram Cereri consecraverunt V Nonas Maias Manilio Fusco II et Domitio Dextro consulibus Titus Flavius Saturninus miles legionis supra scriptae inscripsit et sculpsit Marcus Lucius Iu Caius Annius Caius Valerius V Lucius Stertinius Lucius Perelius Caius Iulius Caius Grasidius Lucius Servilius x Caius Arruntius vi Benius ns Iulius Iovinus Aurelius aiai cohortis secundae lius Donatus Caius Aelius Nervianus Lucius Cornelius Ianuarius Caius Aemilius Saturninus Caius Iulius Honoratus Valerius v Crispius Suc Marcus Aurelius Saturus Ma Venius Consortinus Marcus Clodius Saturus Castra sig(nifer) Marcus Valerius Processus Cillio Caius Iulius Saturninus Castra aen Aug us Rogatianus Cillio cius Miscin cohortis X ius Fusc Castra Lucius Cilius Fortunatus Castra Lucilius Felix Karthagine Octavius Aemeritus sic Castra esius Antoninus Cillio Aelius Fortunatus Cillio Iulius Liberalis Cillio Flavius Ianuarius Cillio item equitum Iulius Donatus Aemilius Lucianus Iulius Saturn Iulius Fundanus Iulius Donatus Flavius sitanus Quintus Clodius Vitalis thina Geminius V Aurelius Iius Munatius Concordius \n
EDCS-63400138.translation: [... for the safety of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus and Julia Mamaea Augusta], and of the entire divine family, they consecrated an altar to Ceres. On the Nones of May, during the consulship of Manilius Fuscus (II) and Domitius Dexter. Titus Flavius Saturninus, soldier of the aforementioned legion, inscribed and carved [this]; Marcus Lucius Iu, Caius Annius, Caius Valerius V, Lucius Stertinius, Lucius Perelius, Caius Julius, Caius Grasidius, Lucius Servilius, Benius ns, Julius Iovinus Aurelius, aiai of the second cohort, lius Donatus, Caius Aelius Nervianus, Lucius Cornelius Ianuarius, Caius Aemilius Saturninus, Caius Julius Honoratus, Valerius v Crispius Suc, Marcus Aurelius Saturus Ma, Venius Consortinus, Marcus Clodius Saturus Castra sig(nifer), Marcus Valerius Processus, Cillio Caius Julius Saturninus Castra aen Aug us Rogatianus Cillio cius Miscin of the tenth cohort, ius Fusc Castra, Lucius Cilius Fortunatus Castra, Lucilius Felix, Karthagine, Octavius Aemeritus sic Castra esius Antoninus Cillio, Aelius Fortunatus Cillio, Julius Liberalis Cillio, Flavius Ianuarius Cillio, also of the equites: Julius Donatus, Aemilius Lucianus, Julius Saturn, Julius Fundanus, Julius Donatus, Flavius sitanus, Quintus Clodius Vitalis, thina, Geminius V, Aurelius Iius Munatius Concordius.
EDCS-63400138.summary: This inscription records the consecration of an altar to Ceres for the safety of Emperor Alexander Severus and Julia Mamaea, listing numerous soldiers from Legio III Augusta and other units, and dating to 225 AD.

EDCS-63400141.text_edition: \n ------ Imperatore Severo Alexandro] Pio Felice Augusto II Aufidio Marcello II consulibus \n
EDCS-63400141.translation: [... during the consulship of Emperor Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus (II) and Aufidius Marcellus (II)].
EDCS-63400141.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning the consulship of Severus Alexander and Aufidius Marcellus, dating to 225 AD.

EDCS-63400142.text_edition: \n et Iuliae [Mamaeae] Augustae matris Augusti nostri totiusque domus d(ivinae) vexill(atio) legionis III Au[gustae piae vindicis Ale][xandrianae] morantes castello Dimmidi sub cura Marci Aureli Avitiani |(centurionis) legionis eiusdem dedicavit\n
EDCS-63400142.translation: [... and to Julia Mamaea Augusta], mother of our Augustus and of the entire divine family, a detachment of the legion III Augusta Pia Vindex Alexandriana, stationed at the fort of Dimmidi, under the command of Marcus Aurelius Avitianus, a centurion of the same legion, dedicated [this].
EDCS-63400142.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a detachment of Legio III Augusta, stationed at Dimmidi, under the command of centurion Marcus Aurelius Avitianus, and likely dedicated to Julia Mamaea, dating from the reign of Alexander Severus (222-235 AD).

EDCS-66200098.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Anulius Pacatus vixit annos LXXXVI\n
EDCS-66200098.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Anulius Pacatus lived for 86 years.
EDCS-66200098.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Quintus Anulius Pacatus.

EDCS-66200099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Caecilius Lupus vixit annos LXXXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-66200099.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Caecilius Lupus lived for 95 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-66200099.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Quintus Caecilius Lupus, who is buried at this location.

EDCS-66300007.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate X consule III patre patriae decreto decurionum pecunia publica Sextus Iulius Maior legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit\n
EDCS-66300007.translation: Under the emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, Sextus Julius Maior, imperial legate for the province with the power of a proconsul and patron of the colony, dedicated this with funds from the state, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-66300007.summary: A dedication of a public work by Sextus Julius Maior under Emperor Hadrian, likely during the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-66300009.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo fortissimo max(imo) tribunicia potestate VII imperatore XI consule II proconsule divi Commodi fratre divi Pii Antonini nepote divi Traiani Parthici abnepote divi Nervae adnepote Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate II Publio Septimio Geta Caesare Augusto Augusti nostri fratre et Iulia Domna Augusta matre castrorum vetustate corruptas ampliato opere excoluit dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum patrono coloniae\n
EDCS-66300009.translation: Under the emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Fortissimus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, emperor for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva; and the emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time; and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, brother of our Augustus; and Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp; [he] improved and enlarged the works, which had been corrupted by age, dedicating it with the participation of Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti and patron of the colony.
EDCS-66300009.summary: An inscription recording the restoration and expansion of public works under Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, with the participation of Quintus Anicius Faustus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-66300014.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Publio Helvio Pertinace Augusto patre patriae tribunicia potestate consule II Lucio Naevio Quadratiano legato Augusti pro praetore a Lambaese milia passuum \n
EDCS-66300014.translation: Under the emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax Augustus, father of the country, with tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time; Lucius Naevius Quadratus, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul, [set up this marker] from Lambaesis, [X] miles.
EDCS-66300014.summary: A milestone inscription marking a distance from Lambaesis, set up by Lucius Naevius Quadratus during the consulship of Pertinax, likely in 193 CE.

EDCS-67100103.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Salustius Urbanus of the 2nd Flavian Cohort of Africans lived for 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-67100103.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, military affiliation, and age at death of Lucius Salustius Urbanus.

EDCS-69100011.summary: A very fragmentary inscription possibly recording the names of individuals associated with Carthage, likely from a funerary or honorary context.

EDCS-69200168.translation: of the municipality of the Cedienses, columns(?) [which X] promised from their generosity [and X] under Victorinus, curator of the state(?).
EDCS-69200168.summary: A fragmentary inscription concerning the erection of columns by the municipality of Cediensium, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE, under the supervision of a curator.

EDCS-70000001.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-70000001.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Diocletian, likely from an official building or monument, dating to his reign (284-305 CE).

EDCS-70000022.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, with tribunician power for the 14th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country. The people of Mophthenses(?) at public expense.
EDCS-70000022.summary: An inscription recording a public work funded by the people of Mophthenses(?) in honor of Emperor Antoninus Pius, dating to around 151 CE.

EDCS-70100123.translation: Emperor [restored name, possibly Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius]
EDCS-70100123.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely commemorating Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, possibly for a dedication or building project.

EDCS-70100155.translation: Sacred to the departed shades. Marcus Abadius Felix, standard-bearer of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious, Victorious, lived 40 years. Marcus Abadius [his name] to his deserving brother.
EDCS-70100155.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Abadius Felix, a standard-bearer of the III Augusta legion, erected by his brother.

EDCS-74100033.translation: Naevia [restored name] lived [restored age] years. She lies here.
EDCS-74100033.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Naevia, recording her life and burial.

EDCS-75100011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Bottia Rogata vixit annos LXXXXV hic sita est\n
EDCS-75100011.translation: Sacred to the departed shades. Bottia Rogata lived 95 years. She lies here.
EDCS-75100011.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bottia Rogata, recording her death at the age of 95.

EDCS-75100012.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Saturius Maximus vixit annos L hic situs est\n
EDCS-75100012.translation: Sacred to the departed shades. Lucius Saturius Maximus lived 50 years. He lies here.
EDCS-75100012.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Saturius Maximus, recording his death at the age of 50.

EDCS-76000019.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, senator of the fatherland, unconquered Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to his divine majesty and might.
EDCS-76000019.summary: This is a dedication by the colony of Thamugadi to Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, likely celebrating his titles and position.

EDCS-78300397.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power 13 times, imperator 6 times, consul 6 times. [Dedicated by] Lucius Acilius Strabo, Gaius Clodius Nummus, imperial legate pro praetore of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-78300397.summary: This is an official dedication honoring Emperor Trajan, listing his titles and consulates, and mentioning his legates Lucius Acilius Strabo and Gaius Clodius Nummus in relation to the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-78300667.translation: Cornelia Urbanilla lies here, having survived a great danger, having lived 27 years, 10 months, 12 days, 9 hours. Tiberius Claudius Vitas to his wife.
EDCS-78300667.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelia Urbanilla, who died at age 27, noting her survival of a great danger and erected by her husband.

EDCS-80600028.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, and of Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrianus, great-great-grandson of the deified Traianus Parthicus and of the deified Nerva, greatest Parthicus, greatest Britannicus, greatest Germanicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power 18 times, imperator 3 times, consul 4 times, father of the fatherland, proconsul. [Dedicated] with public money.
EDCS-80600028.summary: This is an extensive official dedication honoring Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus), detailing his extensive lineage and imperial titles, funded by public money.

EDCS-82300056.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III pater patriae Lucio Vario Ambibulo legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-82300056.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power 16 times, consul 3 times, father of the fatherland. [Dedicated by] Lucius Varius Ambibulus, imperial legate pro praetore of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-82300056.summary: This is an official dedication honoring Emperor Hadrian, listing his titles and consulships, and mentioning his legate Lucius Varius Ambibulus in relation to the III Augusta legion.
